I'd rather leave the retry configuration unchanged by default and modify it only if an environment variable is set, like `BOTO3_MAX_RETRIES`.

Mocket is a socket mock framework, and the _Entry_ class is basically the definition of the protocol you want to mock, and implements its Request and Response classes.
I definitely prefer a simple hook where I can plug a function that creates an _Entry_ instance for each _pook.Mock_, because it's a fundamental concept of Mocket.
It's not a big deal, since we did not reinvent the wheel in both the implementations.

I am looking into it right now, the big thing looks like creating a Mocket _Entry_ every time we define a pook._method_, what tells the engine when it's time to call the fake send[all] and when to use the real _socket_.

Looking briefly at the code, on_welcome will only be called if the server sends a 'welcome' event. I don't see any reason why the IRC client would not be dispatching that event if the server sent it. My suspicion is that the server isn't sending the welcome message, and that the behavior you describe is expected. It would be hard to tell, though, without an actual test case (or at least documentation indicating that we expect the welcome message from the server in all cases).

Here's what I recommend: write a small script (similar to testbot.py) that reproduces the issue with a public server (in a way that doesn't cause a nuisance), run that script to demonstrate that the issue exists, and then post that script here. From there, we can enable some debugging and determine if there's a failure in the library or if it's working as expected.

我说的扩展区如下图。

扩展区有两类，
第一类是主图是股票时，扩展区的内容为股票相关板块的的报价，这部分可以通过各种板块的归属计算得到，但是相对耗时，直接取得数据当然快，但是很复杂。

第二类是主图为指数时，扩展区则是指数的成分股报价，结构简单。我通常是将pytdx和通达信行情软件结合使用，对于板块分析，都是从pytdx开始的。因此，非常在意通达信的版块指数和其成分股。但是，目前，由于很多分析都用到8806开头的指数，而pytdx本地文件板块文件中就没有包含8806这些指数的成分股，所有往往需要手动输入，而且一旦指数增加或成分股变化很难维护。

因此，第一类和第二类是核心问题都是能否获得完成的板块指数清单和板块指数成分股清单。虽然，目前通过本地文件解析可以获得部分内数据，但是从板块数据的完整性上看，如果从扩展区中发现指数成分股应该是一个比较全面完整的办法，我不擅长数据stream的分析，所以无从知道解析这部分数据的难度，故提出问题。

Main changes:

* Converted it all to TypeScript.
* Separated the emulation part from the rendering.

* Code organisation

Can you show me some usage examples of what this would look like?   At a high level, I don't want curio to have a lot of extra plumbing involving objects just to get things running.  Right now, there are two basic modes of operation.  One option is to run a single coroutine.  This creates the kernel, runs the coroutine, shuts down the kernel.

     run(coro)

Or you can submit multiple coroutines to a single kernel:

    with Kernel() as kern:
            kern.run(coro1)
            kern.run(coro2)
            ...

Both are pretty simple in my opinion. What extra thing is the Run class giving me?

`extract` doesn't currently use a gain. Previously, the error array was only ever used for computing a threshold, so no gain was required. If the error array is not given, the threshold is treated as absolute. Here's the section of C code where the threshold is set:

https://github.com/kbarbary/sep/blob/657dc286b16d3e4b27987abd9fae3719ba4816ba/src/extract.c#L232-L252

In `sep_extract()` in C, the gain is available through the first argument, `sep_image* image` (though in `sep.pyx` we need to add a `gain` parameter). In C, you can then add a `gain` parameter to the relevant function in `analyse` and pass `image->gain` to it from `sep_extract()`.

`cvalue` is the "convolved" pixel value when filtering is on. Otherwise it is just the same as `value`. Note that SExtractor uses "convolved" values in places where it seems wrong to do so (but I can't remember concrete examples at the moment.)

Well, according to [this](http://stackoverflow.com/a/27840223/4696215), extracting files from a tar file one by one is as slow as extracting all the files times the number of files, because the files are not indexed. ```python
from pyang import plugin

def pyang_plugin_init():
    plugin.register_plugin(MyYangPlugin())

class MyYangPlugin(plugin.PyangPlugin):
    def add_output_format(self, fmts):
        self.multiple_modules = True
        fmts['my-plugin-output'] = self

    def emit(self, ctx, modules, fd):
        # modules has information on your pyang modules you supplied as input.
        # I just used import pdb; pdb.set_trace(), dir, and print to explore the attributes.
        import pdb
        pdb.set_trace()
        # write your output to fd. this is either stdout or a file but depends on the args sent to the
        #pyang script.
        fd.write('hello world!')

    def add_opts(self, optparser):
        # you can add additional cmd line options here.
        optlist = []
        g = optparser.add_option_group("qsensei-fuse output specific options")
        g.add_options(optlist)
```

Hi! I'm glad you found `greenery` to have some entertainment value. I consider this to be a completed project at the moment, and it's been in "if it ain't broke, don't touch it" mode for several years.

If it does have bugs, then let's fix them. I can think of one case where `x == parse(str(x))` would return `False` but I would be unconcerned, for example when `x` is `charclass('a')` but `parse(str(x))` is a full-blown `pattern` object, `pattern(conc(mult(charclass('ab'), multiplier(bound(1), bound(1)))))`. I don't know if this is the kind of thing you're talking about. But if you have some examples which look more like bugs, we can take a look at fixing them.

I see in one case you've suggested changing a test where parsing `"a.b()()"` would result in a `lego` object where the empty subpatterns had been actively suppressed. In my opinion this *introduces* a `x != str(parse(x))` bug because the empty subpatterns in the original have been removed after a round trip. In general I would prefer to keep parsing and reducing regular expressions as separate operations, and in fact not `reduce`ing the returned `lego` object at parsing time was an intentional decision.

Sorry, but on a second thought, I think we can simplify this greatly. E.g., there's a `check_scoring` function in scikit-learn that we could use to get the "right" `scorer` for a given metric/estimator automatically.

Also, maybe it would be good to rename  `clf` to `estimator`. Then we could add the following docstring for `scoring`:

```
"""
...
    scoring : str, callable, or None (default=None)
        Scoring-function based on scikit-learn:
        - None: Uses the estimator's default scoring function 
        - String (see http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/
                  model_evaluation.html#common-cases-predefined-values)
        - 'misclassification error': 1-accuracy
        - A scorer, callable object / function 
        with signature `scorer(estimator, X, y)`.
...
"""
```

In brief, the `check_scoring` could be used to create the `scorer` like so:

```python
    from sklearn.metrics.scorer import check_scoring
    scorer = check_scoring(estimator, scoring=scoring)
```

Here's a more detailed example demonstrating how this works:

```python
from sklearn.metrics.scorer import check_scoring
from sklearn import datasets
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

iris = datasets.load_iris()
X = iris.data
y = iris.target
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = (train_test_split(X, y,
                                    train_size=0.6, random_state=2))

clf = LogisticRegression(random_state=1)
y_pred = clf.fit(X_train, y_train).predict(X_test)

scorer1 = check_scoring(clf, scoring=None)
score1 = scorer(clf, X_test, y_test)

scorer2 = check_scoring(clf, scoring='accuracy')
score2 = scorer(clf, X_test, y_test)

assert score1 == score2

print('Accuracy', score1)
```

```
Accuracy 0.966666666667
```


And for the misclassification error:

```python
if 'misclassification error':
    scorer3 = check_scoring(clf, scoring='accuracy')
    score3 = 1 - scorer(clf, X_test, y_test)
    print('misclassification error', score3)
```

Sorry about the "change of mind," but I think using `check_scoring` would make things more robust and "cleaner". What do you think?

The only issue I have with GPL licenses is that it makes it hard for companies to use this software. That's because a lot of companies want to package it up themselves in their paid, proprietary apps, and you can't do that using GPL unless you also release all your company source code. That's a big issue for adoption IMO. Public domain licenses are the most effective and permissive ones that exist, which is why I'm a fan of it.

If you really wanna change it to something more 'mainstream', the MIT license is the next best option as it has the same rights, but also includes some more 'business' wording.

FWIW: https://www.rdegges.com/2016/i-dont-give-a-shit-about-licensing/

I saw your discussion in the pull request (https://github.com/biocore/calour/pull/82) and as far as I got it, your only problem is, that you use _Other parameters_ instead of _Other Parameters_. But if that is your only change, you can also just subclass the DocstringProcessor. I had the same issue in my psyplot project where I did want to introduce a new section named "Possible types". But the solution (https://github.com/Chilipp/psyplot/blob/fe1e503b9ebc9c6bd047a86850a4c7bae0b112cf/psyplot/docstring.py#L48) was pretty simple. 

So in your case, the solution would just be to make

```python
from docrep import DocstringProcessor as DocrepDocstringProcessor

class DocstringProcessor(DocrepDocstringProcessor):

    param_like_sections = DocrepDocstringProcessor.param_like_sections + ['Other parameters']

    def get_sections(self, s, base, sections=['Parameters', 'Other parameters']):
        return super(DocstringProcessor, self).get_sections(self, s, base, sections)
```

But as I said, if it is more convenient for you to copy the entire module, please feel free to do so.

Best,
Philipp

> The “.spec file” is hook-keyring.backend.py, right?

No, the .spec file is a file used by [`pyinstaller`](https://github.com/pyinstaller/pyinstaller/wiki) to create a standalone executable from a python script. It contains instructions that are usually right, but sometimes, you have to change a few. In the case of `keyring`, you have to manually import some `keyring` modules to get everything working. I wasn't sure of the benefits of implementing __all__ so I did a little reading.

This is a nice article http://xion.io/post/code/python-all-wild-imports.html

To summarise __all__ doesnt stop the import of private classes, e.g. even if `__all__` was set and didnt specify `_my_private_thing`, you could still do `from my_module import _my_private_thing`.

The benefit to __all__ is when `from my_module import *` is used as it allows you to specify what to import `*` rather than `*` being all internal although it comes with its downside in so much as if there is an incorrect reference in `__all__` it will raise an exception.

Given the size and structure of guizero I dont think implementing __all__ is warranted.

Problem 1: I’ll implement the check for @- entries and remove the key when it is empty as you suggest.

Problem 2: This seems to me potentially to require changing both the recurrence rule and and the @f entry. 

Suppose, for example, that you have the recurring task:

	- weekly @s 2015-07-18 12am @f 20150724T1400;20150718T0000, 20150729T1500;20150725T0000 @r w

where the instance that was due on 7/18 was finished at 2pm on 7/24 and the instance that was due on 7/25 was finished at 3pm on 7/29. 

What result should obtain in the following cases when the user presses delete and selects "this and all subsequent instances"?

Case 1) The (unfinished) instance that is displayed on 8/8 is selected. 

Here it seems pretty obvious that the intent is to delete all repetitions on and after 8/8. This would result in the following modification

	- weekly @s 2015-07-18 12am @f 20150724T1400;20150718T0000, 20150729T1500;20150725T0000 @r w &u 2015-08-07 23:59:00

and, as expected, only three instances would remain: the two finished instances that display on 7/24 and 7/29 and the unfinished instance that displays on 8/1.

Case 2) The (unfinished) instance that is displayed on 8/1 is selected. 

Here too, the desired outcome seems pretty obvious - that all repetitions on and after 8/1 should be removed. The result would be

	- weekly @s 2015-07-18 12am @f 20150724T1400;20150718T0000, 20150729T1500;20150725T0000 @r w &u 2015-07-31 23:59:00 

As expected, only the finished instances that display on 7/24 and 7/29 would remain. The problem is that this item currently can no longer be edited and saved since etm will raise the error “No repetitions were generated”. I’m thinking that the correct behavior in this case would be for etm to remove the recurrence rule itself:

	- weekly @s 2015-07-18 12am @f 20150724T1400;20150718T0000, 20150729T1500;20150725T0000 

Do you agree?

Case 3) The finished instance that was due on 7/25 but is displayed on 7/29 is selected. This is a little less obvious but my guess is that the intent is to remove both the finished instance and all subsequent unfinished instances. If so, then etm needs to remove the recurrence rule and the relevant part of @f:

	- weekly @s 2015-07-18 12am @f 20150724T1400;20150718T0000

leaving only the single finished instance. Do you agree?

Just my two cents,

Maybe a first step that could be convenient just adding something:

```python
def is_valid(self, data):
    try:
        self.validate(data)
        return True
    except SchemaError:
        return False
```

Some time you just not care of what happen and you just want to know if it pass. Then there's the question of what should you do if you want to get the errors after the fact.

Collecting the errors and then returning them is not necessarily a bad idea. Although `validate` became huge enough that the coupling might be difficult to play with.

Love the idea of including `priority` into implicit `Validatable` interface. Here's what I was thinking recently: `validate` is a huge type-conditional on many types and priority is a huge type-conditional—that sounds like a great place for dynamic dispatch. How about: `Schema` constructor walks our schema DSL structure and constructs a proper schema with converting:

* `str` -> `Type(str)`
* `[a]` -> `Iterable([a])`
* `a` -> `Literal(a)`

With each of the `Type, Iterable, Literal` having appropriate `validate` method and `priority` property. We'll be able to get rid of a `priority` function, and split the huge `Schema.validate` into many smaller methods like `Type.validate`. And custom user classes will be able to specify their own priority. At that point we will be able to easily compose priorities, we could say that Optional has priority of it's child, which in turn *might* compute or pick priority from it's children. 

What do you think?

> So the syspath stuff is a little opaque to me right now (and do distutils or setuptools have utility methods to aid with this?).

I'm not sure what you want to know. I wrote a bit about namespace packages below. In general the `sys.path` is generated by a python module called `site.py`. `distutils` and `setuptools` are just tools to push stuff into the `sys.path` (or to add new paths via `.pth` files). 

------

This is the place where namespace packages are tested:
https://github.com/davidhalter/jedi/blob/master/test/test_evaluate/test_namespace_package.py

And this is how a namespace package typically looks like:

https://github.com/davidhalter/jedi/blob/master/test/test_evaluate/namespace_package/ns1/pkg/__init__.py
```
try:
    import pkg_resources
    pkg_resources.declare_namespace(__name__)
except ImportError:
    import pkgutil
    __path__ = pkgutil.extend_path(__path__, __name__)
```

So it uses pkgutil to extend the `__path__` attribute that happens to be an attribute for every module.

Also Jedi doesn't support implicit namespace packages, that have been introduced in Python 3.3, AFAIK. In Python 3.3 you can just skip the `__init__.py` file and you have a namespace package.

I think that this over complicates things, `wal` is meant to be as simple as possible. 

`wal` is really dumb in the sense that it doesn't "know" what colors are. It just repeatedly calls `imagemagick` till it gets 16 colors and then blindly maps those colors to a scheme. `wal` doesn't know if the generated colors will work well together or not it doesn't care. 

`wal` makes various assumptions on the list of colors. It assumes that there'll be enough contrast between color 0 and color 15 to make them background and foreground colors. It assumes that the colors 9-14 will be legible on color 0 as a background.

These assumptions work for 99% of images but there are the images that slip through and provide mediocre results. This is what makes `wal` so great in my eyes, despite it being so simple it gives amazing results.

Now taking only 2 colors from an image is where things will get messy, we can't assume that the scheme we mix with the 2 colors will work well. We can't even assume if the 2 colors from the image will work together as background and foreground colors.

What I can look into is taking one of the 2 colors and generating a palette based on brightness. If the color is red we'll generate a palette that is just reds from dark to light etc. I'm not sure about how well this will work but it should help fill that 1% gap.

This sends the presence too often. I believe, the bug is only triggered if we receive a cached presence from the server and then send our own presence before we are finished processing the vcard ID. Then the server will cache our presence with an empty update element, thereby breaking the avatar for all peers that have not already cached our avatar. If we just receive a presence update from the peer and the hash matches the hash in the vcard we should not send a new presence (since this would be unnecessary additional traffic). Actually, I just noticed, we violate the XEP by not sending an new presence with *empty* `<update>` element *before* we continue to retrieve the vcard and calculate the new hash (see <https://xmpp.org/extensions/xep-0153.html#bizrules-reset>), but I am not sure the prescribed behaviour is really a good idea. Perhaps, we should resend the presence if, and only if, the newly calculated hash does not match the hash in the previous presence (and prehaps additionally if our previously cached hash was None) – but note that this behaviour may trigger a loop (if one client's hash calculation or encoding is buggy and they disagree what the hash of the same image is).

Actually, there is a solution:

1. setns()
2. Popen()
3. fcntl()

But it means that the process will not be able to continue in the main netns — the `setns()` operation can not be undone. So if you need to run a process in the main netns and sometimes spawn child processes in other netns, there should be some specific API implemented to cover your usecase.

One possible way would be to provide file operations directly on NSPopen() «file» attributes, like `proc.stdout.fcntl()`. If it is ok for you, I can try to do so.

Several notes, as the patch is provided:

1. `setns` sets netns for the whole process, you do not need to use NSPopen after it, simple Popen will be enough.
2. if you need to keep the process in the main netns, while running children in some other netns, you can use NSPopen then

If NSPopen object has any of stderr, stdin, stdout objects not None, they have all the file methods as well as fcntl methods. It is possible to run like that:

```
proc = NSPopen('test', ['cat'], stdout=subprocess.PIPE)
flags = proc.stdout.fcntl(fcntl.F_GETFL)
```

I'm not closing the bug since it has to be documented still.

The man page for `openssl mime` says you can pass a raw, DER-encoded PKCS#7 structure using `openssl -inform der -in tmp`. This would at least rule out any encoding issues adding the problem, although my guess is that is unlikely.

It is certainly possible there is an error in one of the CMS structure definitions. I've used some via oscrypto because `.p12` files use PKCS#7 to encrypt key data and certificates, but I'm sure there are some variations that haven't been hit before.

What may be helpful in debugging is to pass a known good file into asn1crypto and use the `.debug()` function to dump the complete structure and all of the encoding details. Comparing this to what is being constructed in Python would probably be one of the fastest paths to figuring it out.

In v0.16.x, `on_voice_state_update` passes two `Member` objects, one for `before` and one for `after` the event happened, see the [documentation](http://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#discord.on_voice_state_update).

As per the documentation (again), you'll notice that there are a bunch of properties such as `voice_channel` on a `Member` object ([see here](http://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#discord.Member.voice) and [here](http://discordpy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api.html#discord.VoiceState.voice_channel)).

The rest seems to be a logic error on your part (and invalid Python due to `NameError`).

To fix your issue you have to check if the member's voice channel before was `None` and if afterwards it isn't. So something like this: 

```py
async def on_voice_update(before, after):
    if before.voice_channel is None and after.voice_channel is not None:
        await client.send_message(..., "hello you joined voice")
```

I made the pull request above. Here's an expanded explanation of the changes:

Added better control over threads/processes, making them shutdown cleanly before the program terminates.

Switched from the iterator to poll() because using the iterator style will poll indefinitely looking for new messages when none are available. That prevents reaching any line receiving the exit signal, or executing any code to exit the thread cleanly. The only way I've found to get around that is to set consumer_time_ms which just forces a static length consumer lifetime (limiting), or to hold the producer open for a few seconds after the stop_event has been set and send one last message so that the iterator returns one last time allowing the for loop to execute the check for the stop_event (dirty).

The output is otherwise identical AFAICT.

Might be worth having parallel examples with documentation of these limitations. Or examples using assign() instead of subscribe() since I've not seen any assign() examples anywhere.

I have the same issue.
I believe the problem stems from the way ansible (v1) passes the path of the file containing the vault password: it's done in the `base_parser` method in `ansible/utils/__init__.py`:
```
    parser.add_option('--vault-password-file', default=constants.DEFAULT_VAULT_PASSWORD_FILE,
        dest='vault_password_file', help="vault password file")
```
`constants.DEFAULT_VAULT_PASSWORD_FILE` has the correct value (i.e. it has the path that is specified by `vault_password_file` in ansible.cfg) but the problem, I think, is that this code (`base_parser`) is *not* called when one calls ansible from the Python API.
As a result, ansible is unable to decrypt the vault and fails with the error above.

The real issue here is that I need to include a certain set of library files, `tkimg` in order to be able to work with PNG-images on platforms that do not support `Tk 8.6`. However, compiling `tkimg` from source is not possible on most modern Linux systems due to dependency conflicts and doing so on Windows is pretty unworkable too because it depends on Tcl header files and whatnot.

Thus, I have to include the compiled library files for the platforms they are available for. However, I do not want to include all library files for all platforms in all wheel packages, I only want to include a single set. But because it is not an extension that can be compiled upon building the wheel, I have to include the files in the package manually.

What files to select depends on the platform, and then the files can be copied over to the right folder and packaged in the wheel. When using a source distribution, all library files are included still and the code uses path finding to select the correct ones out of the full set during runtime.

I don't know why the files don't get copied in your particular case, and I would certainly like to know so I can fix this issue, but I can't reproduce it: It works fine for me when either using sdist or bdist. For now, I cannot delete the weird hack, because I do not know of a better way to implement this. If I could build the `tkimg` package, I would, but I have tried and failed (landing in apt-get dependency hell).

If you do know a better way of fixing this instead, I would love to hear it, but for now this is the only solution that I know of that works and allows non-Tk 8.6 platforms to work with PNG-images.

I added a commit that changes the default for `frames`: 44bc98245ca02d7d88b31e334069d1aad8934e2b.

I played around a bit and it seems that libsndfile *does* allow setting `frames` larger than the end of the file! So it seems that the documentation ( [SFC_FILE_TRUNCATE](http://www.mega-nerd.com/libsndfile/command.html#SFC_FILE_TRUNCATE)) is wrong ...
However, I don't know if the behavior changed at some point or if it is even platform-specific.
Anyway, I think it's best to just call into libsndfile and wait what happens.
Therefore, I deliberately didn't document what happens if `frames` is larger than the total number of frames.

Regarding the read/write position after `truncate()`:
I think it is quite reasonable to keep it at the new end of the file, as libsndfile seems to do.
If the read/write pointer was initially lower than the requested number of frames, both behaviors would be reasonable. But if it was initially *after* the requested number of frames, I think it's quite strange that the read/write position ends up after the end of the file, where the user probably doesn't want to read/write anything.

BTW, I think Python's behavior isn't really Pythonic in this case, I think they just re-used the behavior of the POSIX functions `truncate()`/`ftruncate()`.

I just looked a little bit close into this ticket. I am very hesitant to use any kind of complex output parsing since it is *very* fragile. I thought about alternative steps to gain the necessary information and would propose doing the following instead:

* Determine if the wc has a specific branch checked out or is detached

  ```
  git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD
  # output is either a branch name or "HEAD" when detached
  ```

* Depending on the exact flag there are two paths, first if `not exact`:

  * Determine the remote of the current branch:

    ```
    git rev-parse --abbrev-ref @{upstream}
    # output is the remote name
    ```

  * Get the url of the remote:

    ```
    git config --get remote.<REMOTE>.url
    ```

  * The result is the remote url + the branch name

* If `exact` is requested:

  * Determine the hash:

    ```
    git rev-parse HEAD
    ```

  * Get all remote names

    ```
    git remote
    ```

  * for each remote name check if the hash is part of the remote:

    ```
    git rev-list --remotes=<REMOTE>
    # search for <HASH> in the output
    ```

    * For the first successful remote the result is the remote url (as determine above) + the hash

This approach would not require any complex parsing. Does that sound reasonable?

By the time the ``__init__`` methods get called, the ``args`` and ``kwargs`` have been processed.

For ``LineIterator`` classes (like ``Indicator`` derived classes) which take ``datas`` as input this happens when creating the object

```python
class MetaLineIterator(LineSeries.__class__):
    def donew(cls, *args, **kwargs):
        _obj, args, kwargs = \
            super(MetaLineIterator, cls).donew(*args, **kwargs)

        # Scan args for datas ... if none are found,
        # use the _owner (to have a clock)
        _obj.datas = \
            [LineSeriesMaker(x) for x in args if isinstance(x, LineRoot)]
   ...
```

``donew`` is invoked by the metaclass machinery when creating the instance.

The ``kwargs`` are actually processed in different parts of the platform. This is because many of the classes are subclasses of MetaParams, but some of the params are parsed later for plotting purposes. The concept is the same. For example for regular params defined with the ``params`` idiom

```python
class MetaParams(MetaBase):
...

    def donew(cls, *args, **kwargs):
        # Create params and set the values from the kwargs
        params = cls.params()
        for pname, pdef in cls.params._getitems():
            setattr(params, pname, kwargs.pop(pname, pdef))
```

But the concept is the same and by the time ``__init__` is called no ``datas`` or ``kwargs`` which match defined ``params`` are left for the instance to consume.

```python

  - Limit
  - Stop
  - StopLimit

  - Being able to disregard the OHLC hierarchy to analyze other data (from this link: http://www.backtrader.com/posts/2016-03-08-escape-from-ohlc-land/escape-from-ohlc-land/?highlight=escape%20from%20ohlc)

Bid/Ask data is converted to OHLC to be able to operate (with the broker) on it. Disregarding OHLC is meant to analyze data but not to operate on it.

The calculation for the `ATR` makes no assumption about the initial prices. The `AverageTrueRange` sets (automatically) an internal minimum period of 2 bars, because it needs a bar in the past. The formula in summary:

  - AverageTrueRange = MovingAverage(TrueRange)
  - TrueRange = TrueHigh - TrueLow
  - TrueHigh = Max(High, Previous Close)
  - TrueLow = Min(Low, Previous Close)

Because a `Previous Close` is needed the `ATR` needs at least 2 bars to start a calculation.

Because of the definition it is clear than: TrueHigh >= TrueLow`

With negative values: `TrueHigh - TrueLow` will be something like: `-0.2 - -0.3 = 0.1`. I.e.: The `TrueRange` is positive which is what's to be expected.

> Could you explain this a bit more.

Sure. When you call `worksheet.add_table()`, you can specify a style name in the options (http://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.io/working_with_tables.html), e.g. 
`sheet.add_table('B3:F7', {'style': 'Table Style Light 11'})`. This style name has to be a built-in style, you can't create your own so you're limited to the color/format choices that Excel gives you (21 light, 28 medium, and 11 dark themes).

It would be nice to be able to specify the format for a new table style, which we create through xlsxwriter, and then specify by name when calling `worksheet.add_table()`.

Adding this functionality involves minimal changes to `Packager._write_styles_file()`, a few small changes to `Styles._write_table_styles()` and the addition of `Workbook.add_table_style()` as a public method (plus documentation and tests).

Hi,

The sample code raises the following error:

```python
TypeError: Unsupported type <type 'list'> in write()
```

Which corresponds to the [documentation](https://xlsxwriter.readthedocs.org/worksheet.html#worksheet-write):

> Finally, if none of these rules are matched then a TypeError exception is raised.

So there isn't a bug here.

As a feature request I'm not in favour of mapping arbitrary data types into Excel as strings. I think that would hide too many user errors.

For example, in the sample that you show, writing an empty list is almost certainly an error and writing it as `'[]'` is probably not what the user wants.

My position is that complex types should be handled by the user since they are in the best position to decide how they should be mapped to Excel's small set of available types.

So I'm afraid that it is "no" to this feature request.

@melvinvermeeren I don't believe breathe currently supports a test for this.  In theory, the test could look something like this:

```py
def test_template_struct_unspecialized():
    # template <class T, class U> struct unspecialized_struct { };
    member_def = TestMemberDef(
        kind='struct', name='unspecialized_struct',
        templateparamlist=[TestParam(type_='class T'), TestParam(type_='class U')]
    )
    signature = find_node(render(member_def), 'desc_signature')
    # print(signature.astext())
    assert signature.astext() == 'template <class T, class U> struct unspecialized_struct'
```

This will fail for a couple of reasons, the more important of which is how breathe does its templates.  My understanding is that two separate nodes are emitted, one for the `template` and one for the `struct unspecialized_struct`, meaning `find_node` will only give you one.

The "right" thing to do here is probably figure out why `memberdef` is used for all of these tests instead of `compounddef` (two separate doxygen types) -- none of the tests are actually member definitions, they're all compound definitions:

```xml
<?xml version='1.0' encoding='UTF-8' standalone='no'?>
<doxygen xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance" xsi:noNamespaceSchemaLocation="compound.xsd" version="1.8.13">
  <compounddef id="structunspecialized__struct" kind="struct" language="C++" prot="public">
    <compoundname>unspecialized_struct</compoundname>
    <includes refid="test_8hpp" local="no">test.hpp</includes>
    <templateparamlist>
      <param>
        <type>class T</type>
      </param>
      <param>
        <type>class U</type>
      </param>
    </templateparamlist>
    <briefdescription>
<para>An unspecialized template struct. </para>    </briefdescription>
    <detaileddescription>
    </detaileddescription>
    <location file="test.hpp" line="3" column="1" bodyfile="test.hpp" bodystart="3" bodyend="3"/>
    <listofallmembers>
    </listofallmembers>
  </compounddef>
</doxygen>
```

I can give you more information if you want, but really the point I'm trying to make here is that the current tests do not support it, and I agree the PR here works well.  In my opinion, since sphinx adopted `pytest`, and the number of tests in `test_renderer` are generally few, **and none of the tests work in Sphinx 1.6 or higher**, a more in depth discussion on revamping the testing framework should take place on a different thread :wink:

I ran into this issue as well, and found a simple way to get binaries working:

- create a directory in your project root ('bin' for example)
- put binaries in the bin directory and make sure they're world-executable 
- edit config.yaml line with `source_directories:` to include your `bin` directory

example `config.yaml`:
```
...
# Build options
build:
  source_directories: lib,bin #  source_directories: lib,bin # a comma delimited list of directories in your project root that contains source to package.
```

One last effort on this topic:

> I think the fact that static type checkers prohibit something like:
> ```
> class C:
>     x: int = ...
>     y: int = ...
> 
> c = C('1', 1.1)
> ```
> is rather good, not bad. 

What if say, over half of the uses of `C` required converting a variable to `int`, and what if that conversion was not as simple as calling a builtin but also required an import from some other module?  This doesn't seem like a question of correctness to me, but rather one of convenience.  Very many classes in the real world perform some conversion of arguments within their `__init__` methods, and unlike validators I don't see a good alternative for those who don't want to perform conversions all over their code instead of in one place.  There's the possibility of casting and re-binding the attributes in `__post_init__`, but that would break static type-checking: for that to work the mypy plugin needs to integrate converter annotations into the `__init__` annotations, which means `dataclasses` needs first class support for converters.

What about having a sentinel mix-in class which is injected into the bases, so that users could just use `isinstance()` and `issubclass()` as usual? I feel that would be more consistent with existing expectations.

Regarding your question about subclassing from `BaseItem` vs. `Item`:

Good question. My initial thoughts were that the Item object contains methods that might not be fitting for a Collection object (i.e. Generally speaking, items shouldn't contain any files other than a few system files, however people often confuse "uploading to a collection" as uploading thousands of files to a single collection item).

But, now that I think of it, I think you have the right idea. Some of the Item methods, Collection objects should definitely have (i.e. modify_metadata). I think subclassing from Item makes sense. We can override any methods if necessary (perhaps restrict uploading to collection items, maybe Collection.download() downloads the whole collection (rather than just the files in the collection item), etc.).

The reason I created a BaseItem object at all is that I liked the idea of separating the data structure of an item from where it gets the data and what it does with it.

For example, I work on another library (https://github.com/jjjake/iamine) that is used for data-mining Archive.org item metadata and search results. I thought it might be useful to generalize some of the internetarchive library a bit more, so I could easily plug it in to something like iamine.

Does that make sense?

At any rate, any suggestions you have are warmly welcome!

Well, `urllib` doesn't implement automatic [transparent] Content-Encoding decompression: https://bugs.python.org/issue1508475. I am not sure how to proceed, but here are two options I see:

1. Use `requests`, which does this automatically
2. Implement decompression manually in the `OAuthRemoteApp.http_request`: https://coderwall.com/p/eyadkg/http-request-response-with-compressed-body-in-python

Given that you can instantiate `OAuthRemoteApp`, the fastest workaround for you would be to override the `http_request` method with the manual decompression implementation. You are welcome to make a PR as well.

@Tedko Probably a bit late but I have created a solution for this.

You could create a table navigation service which tracks the current row and exposes navigation methods to modify the current row's value and sets focus to the row.

Then all you would need to do is create a key binding directive where you could track key down events and fire the exposed methods from the table navigation service, on key up or key down.

I have used a controller to link the service methods to the key binding directive via a configuration object called 'keyDefinitions'.

I hope that this is helpful to somebody.

Thanks for your response.

Sorry if I wasn't clear about "default expiration time". I was talking about the `AWS_S3_MAX_AGE_SECONDS_STATIC` setting, which defaults to one year, not anything to do with the filesystem. When I mentioned matching the "file storage default" I was talking about the `AWS_S3_MAX_AGE_SECONDS` setting in the file storage backend, which defaults to one hour.

Backwards compatibility is certainly a fair point. I agree that it would be bad to have some files with the shorter default and some with the longer default. Fortunately, there's an easy solution to that: people can explicitly set `AWS_S3_MAX_AGE_SECONDS_STATIC` to one year, if they like, or they can run `s3_sync_meta` to adopt the new default. By contrast, there's no good way to recover from setting a cache time that's too long (with regard to users that have already cached the file). I think the issue is serious enough to warrant a backwards-incompatible change, but that's up to you.

You may have missed my second point, which is about how the manifest storage implementation here has a more serious problem. The issue is that unversioned (`myfile.css`) and versioned (`myfile.123456.css`) files need to be cached differently. Just because you're using a manifest backend doesn't mean that the only references to your static files come from your dynamically generated pages. (To be clear, I'm not talking about the difference between the static and manifest backends, I'm talking about the two different kinds of file generated by the manifest backend itself.)

Let's take `robots.txt` as an example. After `collectstatic` is run (with the manifest backend) you'll have two files uploaded, `robots.txt` and `robots.123456.txt`. If for some reason you generate an internal reference to that file then, sure, it will be `robots.123546.txt` and the long cache time will be appropriate. But browsers are looking for `robots.txt`, and a long cache time there would be inappropriate.

That's a trivial example, but there are plenty of other scenarios in which your static files need to be available by their real file name (say, a mobile app that accesses static files directly rather than by fetching HTML pages first). That's why I suggest you adopt the approach of whitenoise (linked above), where the versioned files are cached forever, and the unversioned files are cached for a user-configurable amount of time.

I've been struggling with the same problem. And I found a better solution, I think.
- `flask db migrate` already detects the new "on the fly" databases `transaction` and `activity`, and generates a new migration script to 'officially' create those tables.
- Inside that new migration script, I simply loop through the list of `table_listeners` and execute them. This creates the 6 functions and operators as described in the p-a templates folder. (see `pgsql: \df` and `\do`)
- And finally I call `configure_versioned_classes()` and I looped through the list of `cached_statements`. This adds the necessary triggers to my `__versioned__` tables. (see `psql: select * from information_schema.triggers;`)

The next `flask db migrate` doesn't detect any changes. And when I edit a record through the application, a transaction/activity trail is created. So it all seems good so far...

Here's the complete listing of the migration script. And as I said, I only added the two for-loops.
``` python
"""
Add 'postgresql-audit' support

Revision ID: 4cac610a6d91
Revises: 185801ec6c0e
Create Date: 2018-04-26 12:40:15.979353

"""
from alembic import op
import sqlalchemy as sa
from postgresql_audit.base import versioning_manager, cached_statements
from sqlalchemy.dialects import postgresql

# revision identifiers, used by Alembic.
revision = '4cac610a6d91'
down_revision = '185801ec6c0e'
branch_labels = None
depends_on = None


def upgrade():
    # start of "generated by alembic"
    op.create_table('transaction',
        sa.Column('id', sa.BigInteger(), nullable=False),
        sa.Column('native_transaction_id', sa.BigInteger(), nullable=True),
        sa.Column('issued_at', sa.DateTime(), nullable=True),
        sa.Column('client_addr', postgresql.INET(), nullable=True),
        sa.PrimaryKeyConstraint('id')
    )
    op.create_index(op.f('ix_transaction_native_transaction_id'), 'transaction', ['native_transaction_id'], unique=False)

    op.create_table('activity',
        sa.Column('id', sa.BigInteger(), nullable=False),
        sa.Column('schema_name', sa.Text(), nullable=True),
        sa.Column('table_name', sa.Text(), nullable=True),
        sa.Column('relid', sa.Integer(), nullable=True),
        sa.Column('issued_at', sa.DateTime(), nullable=True),
        sa.Column('native_transaction_id', sa.BigInteger(), nullable=True),
        sa.Column('verb', sa.Text(), nullable=True),
        sa.Column('old_data', postgresql.JSONB(astext_type=sa.Text()), server_default='{}', nullable=True),
        sa.Column('changed_data', postgresql.JSONB(astext_type=sa.Text()), server_default='{}', nullable=True),
        sa.Column('transaction_id', sa.BigInteger(), nullable=True),
        sa.ForeignKeyConstraint(['transaction_id'], ['transaction.id'], ),
        sa.PrimaryKeyConstraint('id')
    )
    # end of "generated by alembic"

    # add 'postgresql-audit' functions and operators
    for table in versioning_manager.table_listeners:
        for trig, event in versioning_manager.table_listeners[table]:
            if isinstance(event, sa.schema.DDL):
                op.execute(event)
            else:
                event('dummy_table_argument', op.get_bind())

    # add __versioned__ table triggers
    versioning_manager.configure_versioned_classes()
    for query in cached_statements:
        event = cached_statements[query]
        if isinstance(event, sa.schema.DDL):
            op.execute(event)
        else:
            event('dummy_table_argument', op.get_bind())

    # or else, an explicit add is possible too, better reflecting the DROP in downgrade()
    ### op.execute("SELECT audit_table('{}');".format('user_def'))


def downgrade():
    # start of "generated by alembic"
    op.execute('DROP TRIGGER audit_trigger_row ON user_def')

    op.drop_table('activity')

    op.drop_index(op.f('ix_transaction_native_transaction_id'), table_name='transaction')
    op.drop_table('transaction')
    # end of "generated by alembic"
```

Hi,

``redmine.role.all()`` returns you all the available roles in Redmine with only ``id`` and ``name`` attributes. If you need to get each role's permissions you have to do this:

```python
roles = redmine.role.to_resource_set([role.refresh() for role in redmine.role.all()])
```

``refresh()`` method on the role resource is a convenient shortcut to ``redmine.role.get(ROLE_ID)``, that means that to get each role's permissions you have to make a separate request to Redmine for each role. This is not a python-redmine's limitation, this is how Redmine's REST API works and there is nothing we can do about that.

Hope that helps.

```python
with usb1.USBContext() as context:

wow, shocked you found it!

> 
> What does your modified Weibull model look like?

the basic model is simple – the "CDF" is `c * (1 - exp(-(lambda*t)^k))`. It's not a real CDF because it obviously doesn't converge to 1.0

it gets a bit more complicated though... `c` is then modeled using a logistic regression (with unknown parameters `beta`) and `lambda` is the `exp(...)` of a linear combination of unknown parameters `alpha`). Fitting `k` as a single scalar to make it simple. Not sure if this model makes sense but seems to fit things decent for my datasets.

> Lifelines doesn't have weibull-regression, so what you are adding is novel. It would be nice to eventually add to lifelines. If I understand your use-case correctly, the configuration you need is:

``` py
# The university username unique identifies the model in both the database and LDAP.
LDAP_AUTH_USER_LOOKUP_FIELDS = ("university_username",)

# Map model fields to LDAP fields.
LDAP_AUTH_USER_FIELDS = {
    "email": "mail",
    "university_username": "uid"
}
```

You then call:

``` py
authenticate(university_username="foo", password="bar")
```

Which will then call:

``` py
connection(university_username="foo", password="bar")
```

Which will then attempt to bind to the ldap server with:

``` py
ldap3.Connection(ldap3.Server(settings.LDAP_AUTH_URL), user="uid=foo", password="bar". auto_bind=auto_bind)
```

Let's take one thing at a time.

You can create a script without default configs and set some them as required with `REQUIRED_SETTINGS`, e.g.:

```yaml
# s.yaml
SIMPLE_SETTINGS:
  REQUIRED_SETTINGS:
    - TeamcityBuildBranch
```

```python
# app.py
from simple_settings import settings

try:
    settings.setup()
except ValueError as e:
    print(e)
```

```
$ SIMPLE_SETTINGS=s.yaml python app.py
The following settings are required: TeamcityBuildBranch
```

But you can't set value for this config only with an environment variable:
```
$ SIMPLE_SETTINGS=s.yaml TeamcityBuildBranch=foo python app.py
The following settings are required: TeamcityBuildBranch
```

Even if you use `OVERRIDE_BY_ENV` special settings:
```yaml
# s.yaml
SIMPLE_SETTINGS:
  OVERRIDE_BY_ENV: True
  REQUIRED_SETTINGS:
    - TeamcityBuildBranch
```
```
$ SIMPLE_SETTINGS=s.yaml TeamcityBuildBranch=foo python app.py
The following settings are required: TeamcityBuildBranch
```
Maybe ["load multiple settings modules"](http://simple-settings.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#load-multiple-settings-modules) can helping you, e.g:

```yaml
# s.yaml
SIMPLE_SETTINGS:
  REQUIRED_SETTINGS:
    - TeamcityBuildBranch
```
```yaml
# x.yaml
TeamcityBuildBranch: "Foo"
```
```python
# app.py
from simple_settings import settings

try:
    settings.setup()
except ValueError as e:
    print(e)

print(settings.TeamcityBuildBranch)
```
```
$ SIMPLE_SETTINGS=s.yaml,x.yaml python app.py
Foo
```
(Another way is to create something like "settings validators", but it's a feature request 😄 )

The problem in this case is not the GIL (at least not the main problem). The issue is with eventlet's cooperative multitasking, which requires all blocking functions in the Python library to be patched. If you have done the monkey patching and you still get this, then the inotify package must be using functions from the library for which eventlet does not have patched versions.

There is a `eventlet_inotifyx` package that makes package inotifyx compatible with eventlet: https://pypi.python.org/pypi/eventlet_inotifyx. Maybe that will work.

If everything else fails, I think the advice in the snippet of README you copied is sound. Put this in another process, so then you are not required to use eventlet in that process, and everything will work.

Finally had a chance to look at this properly.

I don't see a really _clean_ way of supporting save/create/update.

None of these methods have access to the initial data, only validated data. So we'd either need to:
   * _assume_ `self.get_deserializer_for_data(self.initial_data)` will return the right thing (noting that it's possible that `initial_data` might be unrelated to the validated data if something else in the hierarchy has messed with it)
   * ignore the data. Determine serializer by calling `get_serializer_for_instance(instance)` from the `update()` method. This doesn't work for `create` since there is no `instance` arg. Also this is a bit unintuitive since you probably only expect that method to get called when serializing outgoing models, not when saving incoming ones.
   * When `get_deserializer_for_data` is called the first time to process the incoming data, remember what serializer was chosen, and use it again when saving the model instance. This is possibly the best option but it's not clear where we can save the serializer that will be robust against unforeseen use-cases.
   * Add a third method `get_serializer_for_validated_data`. Yuck.

If you're happy to merge without this, I think that'd be best for now.

`Field` name attributes have been refactored as I've proposed above. Some additional changes that I've made in the process:
- Added new method `Field.bind(table)` which takes care of all the boilerplate for setting references to the new parent table.
- `Field.clone()` now unsets references to parent table and database so that `bind()` can do some extra validation.
- TFK foreign keys (multi-field references to legacy tables) were completely broken, that particular branch of migrator code was apparently never tested. They work now, but the code should be merged with normal single-field foreign keys. It's literally the same thing, just with a few extra field names fed to the template. Many dialects don't even have a `'reference TFK'` template string. I've also added a unit test for TFK foreign keys which skips any adapter that does not have the template string. Remove the skip condition after you merge TFK with normal foreign keys.
- I took the liberty to fix issue #430

    val init = new Xavier()

    logger.info(s"symbol shape: $shape")
    val providedData = trainIter.provideData
    val providedLabel = trainIter.provideLabel
    logger.info("providedData: " + providedData.mkString)
    logger.info("providedLabel: " + providedLabel.mkString)
    mod.bind(dataShapes  = trainIter.provideData, labelShapes = Some(trainIter.provideLabel))
    logger.info("bound")
    mod.initParams(init)
    logger.info("initialized")
    mod.initOptimizer(optimizer = optimizer)
    logger.info("optimizer initialized")
    val outShapes = mod.outputShapes
    logger.info(s"OutShapes (module): $outShapes")

    for (epoch <- 0 until numEpoch) {
      while (trainIter.hasNext) {
        val batch = trainIter.next()
        mod.forward(batch)
        mod.updateMetric(metric, batch.label)
        mod.backward()
        mod.update()
      }
    }

@sebradloff Sorry for late reply. 

In its current form, the MesosExecutor allows specifying static values for CPU & memory per task. When the airflow framework scheduler receives a resource offer from mesos master that has less resources than what is specified in this config, it rejects that offer. Or in other words, it accepts only those offers that have more or equal number of resources as mentioned in the config. But its a STATIC value. Same for all tasks for all DAGs. 
Now I'll preface this by saying I'm pretty new to airflow and specifically to the scheduler-foo logic but FWIK having able to specify cpu & memory per task for a DAG would require changes in airflow scheduler's code where it's creating `TaskInstance`s but it sounds like a suggestion worth exploring. 

Regarding having to specify both `docker` & `mesos` containerizers it looks like you are right. We need only one in this case - the `docker` one. Although I'm yet to try that out on my setup. Not getting enough bandwidth :( 

Thanks for the architectural diagram. I did create a [basic architectural diagram](http://agrajmangal.in/blog/wp-content/uploads/2018/01/airflow_mesos.png) in [my first blog post](http://agrajmangal.in/blog/big-data/running-airflow-on-top-of-apache-mesos/) on this subject - See http://agrajmangal.in/blog/wp-content/uploads/2018/01/airflow_mesos.png But I agree specifying more details like containers inside executors and webserver and scheduler also running on mesos slaves makes sense. Will update the blog post as soon as I find some time.

Personally I prefer not having a workflow enabled. One reason is that a test setup should be as minimal as possible in my opinion, to keep things simple and maintainable (it is very hard to remove things at a later point once they have been added). Another reason is that an enabled workflow often requires additional code in tests that make them less readable (e.g. when you have to publish every single content object you create). I just prefer to have more control over what I'm doing.

Though, I'd be ok with keeping the workflow enabled because this is what people expect.

We should still clean up the p.a.contenttypes/p.a.event test fixtures. Those two packages are currently good examples of how test fixtures should not look like in my opinion (global mimetypes, test-folder, z2 installs, multiple if-else, timezones, etc.). 

The problem is that lots of packages rely on those fixtures. The longer we wait, the harder it gets to clean up things. There are a few test isolation problems that might have something to do with the p.a.contenttypes/p.a.event layers. Though, those are very hard to debug if your package test relies on p.a.contenttypes, which relies on p.a.event and both packages do lots of things in their test fixtures.

With the commit https://github.com/xbmc/addon-check/pull/64/commits/cd194c4e687c591ab911e8e33e64401085966919, reporters are injected and managed automatically. A new configuration `reporter` is added to both `.test-config.json` and command line arguments which are used to enable reporters.

By default, Console reporter is enabled (`@reporter(name="console", enabled=True)`). If you provide `"reporter": []` in `.test-config.json` as below, it will disable all reporters.
```
{
    "check_legacy_language_path": true,
    "check_legacy_strings_xml": true,
    "check_license_file_exists": true,
    "reporter": []
}
```

The user can override that option in the command line by providing `--reporter console`. The reporter name `console` is determined by the decorator `@reporter(name='console'...)`. Therefore, if you add a new reporter, you just need to define the name and whether it is enabled by default.

```
$ kodi-addon-checker --help
usage: kodi-addon-checker [-h] [--version] [--reporter {console}]
                          [add-on [add-on ...]]

Checks Kodi repo for best practices and creates problem and warning reports.
If optional add-on directories are provided, check only those add-ons.
Otherwise, scan current repository and check all add-ons in the current
directory.

positional arguments:
  add-on                optional add-on directories

optional arguments:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
  --version             show program's version number and exit
  --reporter {console}  enable a reporter with the given id(s). You can put
                        this option multiple time

```
If there are more than one reporters (say `console` and `github`) user can enable both of them using the following command:
```
kodi-addon-checker --reporter console --reporter github
```

> I disagree with this. We don't pin any other dependencies just assuming
> they are going to break everything on a major release, and in general
> that's not common practice in Python due to the flat package namespace.
> Plus, the whole point of the split is that there are *stable* APIs
> between our various components, and they should be able to make *major*
> releases independently of each other. Since Python doesn't do
> javascript-style package installation, stable means over time, not just
> version numbers. If we are doing lock-step 4->5 of everything, there wasn't
> much of a point to separating the repos.
>
Good point. For package that sit at the bottom/mid of our dependencies,
this makes sense. But the reason we did the repo split was the enable
higher level components (ipywidgets) to move quickly and break things.
Traitlets is challenging because it is at the very bottom, but also used
extensively by ipywidgets in advanced ways that require its evolution (not
breakage necessarily though).



> We can't have people using 4.0 of the notebook with a 5.0 of
> ipywidgets/traitlets that are API breaking...Is this what you were thinking
> about?
> But I don't see why ipywidgets and notebook future versions couldn't start
> to both depend on an API breaking traitlets before 2 years.
>
> One package depending on a breaking change means forcing a release of a
> dozen packages before it can be used - we use traitlets *everywhere*. A
> breaking change in traitlets means forcing a new release of every package
> we maintain. If, however, there's a new API that doesn't break the old one,
> we can start using it as soon as it's available without issue, which is the
> current state of this repo. The new API has been added next to the old one,
> and we can keep it there to allow a smooth transition. The only reason now
> to *remove* the old API is cleanliness, which isn't good enough anymore.
> We shouldn't break old APIs unless a strong case can be made for the direct
> benefit of its removal, even with new major releases. This is something we
> have historically been very bad at, and traitlets being the bottom of the
> stack makes it the single most important place to be careful. Two years
> might be an exaggeration, but I don't think one is.
>
I full agree that in this case we can make traitlets fully BW compat and we
should. Even though we don't want to be forced to do lock-step releases of
all packages, that doesn't mean we *can't*, when needed. We are not there
yet, but commiting to 2 years of no API breaks feels like an overreaching
and unrealistic one given the fast pace of the project. If we find that N
months from now everything is being held up by our inability to move APIs
forward, then we could do another synchronized release of a larger subset
of project.


> My main point: due to the repo split, our deprecations cycles need to get
> a lot longer.
>
For foundational package that rely on each other, such as traitlets,
message spec, nbformat etc. I agree. I think I am mostly reacting possibly
over-generalized idea of "no API breakage for 2 years". We still have a lot
of things to break in parts of our code base...

Cheers,

Brian

A ValidationError is raised on the first encountered error. The context captures the location of the error in an arbitrarily nested input structure, for example:
```python
import valideer as V
v = V.parse({"foo": ["integer"]})
try:
    v.validate({"foo": [1,"2","3"], "bar": 3.14})
except V.ValidationError as ex:
    print ex
    print ex.context

# prints
Invalid value '2' (str): must be integer (at foo[1])
[1, 'foo']
```

So it is not possible to capture all individual errors at the moment. The main use case of valideer is validation and adaptation for complex nested JSON API requests where the consumer is typically a developer, not so much web form validation for reporting all individual errors to end-users. I will leave this issue open as a feature request though I'm not sure how easy it would be to add in a backwards-compatible way. 

Until then what you could try is, instead of definining a single validator for the whole form, define one validator for each form field and then manually loop over them, something like this:
```python
field_validators = {
    "foo": V.parse("integer"),
    "bar": V.parse(["string"]),
}

form_data ={
    "foo": "3",
    "bar": ["1", 2, 3, "4"],
}

errors = []
for key, value in form_data.items():
    try:
        field_validators[key].validate(value)
    except V.ValidationError as ex:
        errors.append("Error at '{}': {}".format(key, ex))
```

I wanted to quickly add some notes that may be helpful in the future for someone that wants to implement HTTP/2 in waitress, as well as expectations I have for what that implementation looks like, and what I'd like to see if this were to be implemented for waitress.

1. Waitress is a pure Python implementation, if HTTP2 is added, I would accept it as a default option if and only if it too is a pyre Python implementation. I'd prefer not to have to rely on other libraries, but if they are Python only it's not that bad, but no C code at all.
2. There are some assumptions made in the code right now that a single `HTTPChannel` is associated with zero or one currently executing task (multiple requests can be queued up, and in a loop a task will be used to answer those requests). To take advantage of the fact that HTTP/2 allows multiple requests to be executed at the same time, that limitation would need to be removed. This also means that we need limitations on how many threads can be used by a single HTTP connection (a similar request is made in #88 but that is limiting how many connections a single client can make)
3. We would want to introduce some sort of API that allows HTTP/2 push to be used. I like the idea of making it something like Cloudflare's `Link` support whereby it does a secondary request for the resource to be pushed.

Django isn't matching `clone/` with `url(r'^(.+)/change/clone/$')`.  The browser takes `clone/` and appends it to the url of the current page.  Running the sample project with Django 1.10, the Change Post page is at `/admin/posts/post/1/change/` which makes the 'Clone It!' link have a path of
`/admin/posts/post/1/change/clone`

@techalchemy removal of `pipenv run` removed real motivation of my PR.

You might be aware of common misconception and confusion about "starting virtualenv" - after learning about activating virtualenv, people do not understand, it only simplifies calls to underlying python interpreter within that virtualenv and mistakenly expect some special magic. This results in attempts to run activation script before using scripts from the virtualenv even though it would be sufficient to simply call the python interpreter using absolute path.

The situation in `tox.ini` is similar, `tox` user is used to install some python libraries into virtualenv and then simply use it. Adding `pipenv run <command>` gives false impression, that commands installed via `pipenv` shall be magically run via `pipenv run` even though it is not true.

Imagine a test suite, which has couple of test cases, where are called python package provided CLI commands. Do we expect them to be modified to `pipenv run <command>` variant?

Definitely not, but man page is currently contributing to such misunderstanding.

> the current solution supports both, the legacy and the future, yet one might need to exclude additional paths.

What I meant was that if the PEP 420 `find_packages` ends up creating a lot of extra work for non-namespace packages who now have to write `find_packages(exclude=['tests/', 'scripts/']` or something when they didn't have to before, then I don't think this is a real improvement.

The default should be the thing that works correctly in the common case, with an easy upgrade path for the uncommon case. If 1% of packages use implicit namespaces (I think this number is probably a dramatic over-estimate), and 20% have a `tests/` and/or `scripts/` file with `.py` files in them, that means we are making 20% of people write:

`find_packages(exclude=['scripts/', 'test'])`

Just so that 1% of people don't have to write:

`find_packages(namespace=True)`

We're also at a pretty significant risk of having a bunch of packages not realize that this has happened and accidentally installing `scripts` and `tests` packages into the root namespace!

I'm pretty sure this isn't a Setuptools bug. It's possibly a bug with Setuptools-scm or maybe with sshuttle. My guess is that you're trying to run setup.py on a copy of the code without the scm metadata and without the version info that would have come with an sdist.

It is worth noting that the build process is being run in a private network namespace, with no access to outside systems. Fetching packages from pypi should fail in this case (not produce an infinite recursion).

Looks like with Python 3 (or at least 3.4, 3.5, 3.6, I don't have 3.3 to test), `TestLoader.loadTestsFromModule` already automatically recurse into sub-modules, so tests are duplicated. I guess one way to fix it would be to [keep track of visited modules](https://github.com/benoit-pierre/setuptools/compare/tests_releated_fixes_and_tweaks...benoit-pierre:fix_701#diff-167324766965f8feb7a26f2767258466R20).

To fix the fact that tests are not run with Python 2, [adding `discover` to unittest arguments](https://github.com/benoit-pierre/setuptools/compare/tests_releated_fixes_and_tweaks...benoit-pierre:fix_701#diff-167324766965f8feb7a26f2767258466R112) when no testsuite is specified fix the issue, except for Python 2.6.

What you're describing here needs to be addressed at a higher level, as a PEP or at least discussion within distutils-sig. It's not sufficient to have supply a `requires_alternative()` to an `install_requires` directive because that directive is run when setup.py is invoked, which happens during builds, including wheel (binary) builds. These builds would then resolve their requirements relative to the build environment and not the user's target environment.

What you need instead is a specification on how alternative requirements might be declared and guidance on how builders and installers should deal with such requirements. I think there are already efforts underway in this vein, not only dealing with alternatives, but also dealing with packages that supersede others or satisfy a requirement of another (e.g. Pillow satisfying PIL).

As it stands, this request isn't actionable here. Thanks for the suggestion and do feel free to pursue this with the community.

Even though they are both zipfiles, wheels and ```pyz``` archives are different (specifically, the wheel format doesn't allow for a top-level ```__main__.py``` file). See the wheel PEP, specifcally https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0427/#is-it-possible-to-import-python-code-directly-from-a-wheel-file, for a discussion of why executing directly from a wheel is viewed as a nice, but not formally supported, property.

On the specific note of the internal setuptools and pip wheels, those are embedded zipfiles within the virtualenv wheel, and using them inplace is far more complex than it maybe seems at first glance.

Hi @adamn, the setting for date preference for future or past is applicable to incomplete dates. Take a look at the examples below:

```
In [3]: parse('23 November')
Out[3]: datetime.datetime(2016, 11, 23, 0, 0)

In [4]: parse('23 November', settings={'PREFER_DATES_FROM': 'past'})
Out[4]: datetime.datetime(2015, 11, 23, 0, 0)

In [5]: parse('Saturday')
Out[5]: datetime.datetime(2016, 4, 30, 0, 0)

In [6]: parse('Saturday', settings={'PREFER_DATES_FROM': 'future'})
Out[5]: datetime.datetime(2016, 5, 7, 0, 0)
```

In your sample, the date is complete and the issue is with ordering where day comes first. You can fix this issue by specifying a date format:

```
In [8]: parse('9/2/16')
Out[8]: datetime.datetime(2016, 9, 2, 0, 0)

In [9]: parse('9/2/16', date_formats=['%d/%m/%y'])
Out[9]: datetime.datetime(2016, 2, 9, 0, 0)
```

The simplest thing to do:

1.  Create a jar using `lein uberjar`
2.  `scp` that jar *and* your topology Clojure file—I'm assuming this is called `events.clj`—to a server on AWS you can submit from.
3.  SSH into the same server and run:

```sh
bin/storm jar events-0.0.1-SNAPSHOT-STANDALONE.jar streamparse.commands.submit_topology events.clj
```

Please let us know if that doesn't work.

I think the issue is that HortonWorks has their own versions of Storm that are separate from the official Apache ones.

You should just be able to add

```clj
:repositories {"HDP Releases" "http://repo.hortonworks.com/content/repositories/releases"}
```

to your `project.clj` and then set the Storm version to be exactly what they use.

I like `(y, x)` better because it can be used directly for array indexing.  In principle, one could argue that the aperture `positions` should also be in `(y, x)` axis order.  It would be nice to have a consistent API that would also work with array indexing, but perhaps changing everything to `(y, x)` will cause too much confusion?

Using a `namedtuple` has the advantage of making `x` and `y` explicit, which is nice, at the expense of making direct array indexing a little more cumbersome.  Even so, that's probably better than having to flip an `(x, y)` index (or remember that you *need* to flip `x` and `y`).

Does anyone else have other thoughts before I change this PR to use `namedtuple`s?

@JDWarner I actually did a fair bit of reading before agreeing with @kevin-keraudren's approach. NumPy's approach is correct in the sense that it guarantees that rounding errors won't "drift" when applied repeatedly:

"""
Algebraic operations covered by IEEE 754, namely + , - , · , / , ÷ and Binary <-> Decimal Conversion with
rare exceptions, must be Correctly Rounded to the precision of the operation’s destination **unless the programmer has specified a rounding other than the default.**
[...]
Besides its lack of statistical bias, this choice has a subtle advantage; it prevents prolonged drift during slowly convergent iterations containing steps like these:
"""

(Emphasis mine; see page 3 of [this document](http://www.cs.berkeley.edu/~wkahan/ieee754status/IEEE754.PDF))

There are other cases when a different rounding scheme (e.g. away from 0) may be desirable, e.g. when we are likely to want consistently-spaced integers from a rounding operation of consistently-spaced half-integers. I think this is one of those cases.

@scottpurdy

Discussed offline and my recommendations are:

1. Move the logic in `segmentUpdateWalk` into separate functions (like `getSegmentsBeforeColumn` that is called for both active and matching segments and returns the set of segments before the specified column, and then a similar `getSegmentsForColumn`). This makes `segmentUpdateWalk` an "imperative shell" [1] with the new functions comprising the "functional core" [1].

2. Move the logic for each lambda into a named function. These functions will take additional parameters for the variables needed from the `compute` scope. In order to avoid passing these additional parameters to `segmentUpdateWalk`, you can use `std::bind` as you had suggested.

There are two additional possible changes. I haven't decided if I think they are better or worse than what we have.

A. Move the outer loop in `segmentUpdateWalk` into `compute` and get rid of `segmentUpdateWalk` completely. This eliminates the need for passing functions around, creating a flatter stack trace, but it requires that `compute` understand some of logic around `activeColumns`, `activeSegments`, and `matchingSegments`.

B. Move the logic functions extracted from `segmentUpdateWalk` (in recommendation 1 at the very beginning of this comment), along with the corresponding data structures, into a separate class that is responsible for understanding the structure of the data structes and the relationship between them. We probably *don't* want to do `A` if we do this change.

[1] [Boundaries](https://www.destroyallsoftware.com/talks/boundaries)

This stems from the fact that nested expressions weren't first-class citizens in the original query parser, so to speak.  While expressions like "foo AND NOT bar" can be reduced to linear L2R processing by transforming them to "foo -bar", expressions like "foo OR NOT bar" require an "invisible" parentheses like "foo OR (*everything* NOT bar)".

This should not be a problem in Invenio 2.1 code base thanks to the invenio-query-parser package where nested expressions are first-class citizens, so to speak (untested).  In the legacy code base we can fix the problem by introducing special treatment for "OR NOT".  I can take care of this bug for maint-1.2 as well, because this affects last stable release too.

Awesome! Getting a development environment up to hack away at objection is pretty simple. All you need to do is clone your fork from Github and install it using `pip`. For example:

- Fork the repository on Github
- Clone it somewhere on disk like at `~/code/objection`
- Create a new python3 virtual environment with something like `virtualenv -p python3 ~/code/venv3`
- Activate the new virtual environment with `source ~/code/venv3/bin/activate`
- `cd` to your fork at `~/code/objection` and install it with `pip install --editable .`

Now, any changes you make will be immediately applied without the need for another install.

Have fun!

The problem is that boost is trying to use the C++ stl, which is not available in the NDK. If you look in the user-config.jam included by the recipe, it actually tries to get this include from `<compileflags>-I$(ANDROIDNDK)/sources/cxx-stl/gnu-libstdc++/$(TOOLCHAIN_VERSION)/include`, a path which exists only in the [CrystaX NDK](https://www.crystax.net/android/ndk). So, that should be the solution to this issue, use that NDK (and be careful to fix the rest of the recipe to also work, e.g. make sure the TOOLCHAIN_VERSION env var used by the recipe is set).

Actually, the way we used of the regression is quite different.
In my previous experience, we use regression as follows.

For a single factor model regression, 
```
Y = alpha + beta * X
Y = An array of returns consists of all stocks (the dependent variable)
X = An array of normalized factors consists of all stocks (the independent variable1)
```

For a multi-factor model regression, 
```
Y = alpha + beta1 * X1 + beta2 * X2
Y = An array of returns consists of all stocks (the dependent variable)
X1 = An array of normalized factors (factor 1) consists of all stocks (the independent variable1)
X2 = An array of normalized factors (factor 2) consists of all stocks (the independent variable2)

```
Using the 2-factor model, we can get the knowledge of what level that factor 1 and factor 2 explain return together. The Adjust R-squared is normally higher than single-factor model(When there is no multi-collinearity we may think the model is better than a single-factor model)

You can see the way in 
[http://www.investopedia.com/terms/m/multifactor-model.asp](url)

I haven't figured out how to do this multi-factor model in the way alphalens used.
Alphalens use a regression, where dependent variable is calculated by a weighted return using a single factor. I have no idea how to handle this settings in a multi-factor scenario.

I don't understand what you want to do here.  It would be helpful if you provided a strawman example of what you want to do.

If you just want to have your own custom code that reads in some new top level field in the YAML file and does something with it, including using some of the config functionality, that's certainly possible.  You can look at [demo8](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/blob/master/examples/demo8.py) for an example of a python script the runs aspects of the config parser.

If you want to use the regular `galsim` executable to do mostly regular config parsing, but with some custom code for particular aspects of it, that's also possible, although only on master at the moment, and soon in version 1.4.  See some of the yaml files in examples/des.  e.g. [blend.yaml](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/blob/master/examples/des/blend.yaml) and [meds.yaml](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/blob/master/examples/des/meds.yaml) in particular.  There is also documentation about writing these cutom types in the v1.4 config documentation pages: e.g. [Value types](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/wiki/Config-Values#custom-value-types), [Output types](https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/wiki/Config-Output#custom-output-types), etc.

If neither of these are what you meant, please provide a straw man yaml file for what you have in mind and I'll see if I can give you advice about how to make it happen.
