>  Is this a sign that the ceil and floor should be the opposite of what they currently are? 

I don't think so.  Probably it's a sign that something slightly different from the current code should be done, but if you reverse them you will run into problems.  The goal of these statements as I recall (it's been a long time since I dug into this part of the code) is to identify a region lying within an ellipse of some radius set by ```max_moment_nsig2``` and orientation; if you swap them, you're outside the ellipse which by definition is not what you want.

Yes.  GalSim works with Python 3.4, 3.5 and 3.6.  (All regularly tested on Travis.)  I don't use Homebrew, so I can't speak to the second question, but I suspect not.  I don't think it has even been updated to version 1.4.  Pinging @dstndstn who wrote the existing brew.

The code currently assumes that the RealGalaxy images are large enough to cover the full extend of the blended postage stamp.  You could certainly do something more sophisticated to make sure that they do in fact cover the whole area (by using `noise_pad_size`) or to keep track of the extent of each postage stamp on the larger image and whitening each region separately.  Probably the first option would be easier.

But these enhancements wouldn't require any changes to the overall config structure.  The point of this exercise from my perspective was to make sure that the hooks I put into the config parser were sufficient to handle this kind of simulation.  Please do feel free to take this as a straw man and improve upon it.  :)

And yes, the current hooks use a single PSF for everything in a single stamp.  If you wanted a variable PSF within a branch, I suppose that would be doable.  But currently, the code parses the `psf` field prior to calling the code to build the "Profile" (which is multiple profiles in this case).  This is what the `psf` argument is for `ProfileBlend`, which we use for all the profiles in the blend.  So to get a variable PSF I guess you'd have to do some custom parsing of the psf field within the Profile function.

The way this works in gnome-terminal and a few other vte-based ones is:

- They're based on libvte-2.91 (gtk3) which supports the OSC 7 escape sequence that sets the terminal emulator's notion of the working directory. That is, no more digging under /proc at all, the shell tells it explicitly to the terminal emulator (as a string, potentially containing symlink components).

- Source /etc/profile.d/vte-2.91.sh at startup so that it's hooked up to the shell.

- Set the actual working directory as well as $PWD in the newly opened tabs, the latter one makes the shell keep symlink components.

Your first try can be solved by wrapping

```bash
def main() {
    if [[ "$1" == "--auto" ]] ; then
        shift
        declare -A args
        eval "$(docopt_auto_parse "${BASH_SOURCE[1]}" "$@")"
    fi
}
````

And then, your last line will simply be a call to `main` and you can keep the order of function definitions you want.

I pushed some new commits.  I was originally going to send this as a follow up PR to keep the scope down, but it helps explain some of the changes in this PR.

Basically, I removed the whole `DEPLOY_PHASE` idea.  Instead, we load the deployed values from deployed.json in the case of an update, or we take the values directly from the return values of an API call.  These values are then loaded and stored as variables.  This makes all the cases consistent (create vs. update) as well as cases where the only way to knew the deployed value is from the deployed.json.  For example, you don't know the appropriate rest_api_id because Rest APIs can have identical names, so there's nothing in the API you can query to know for sure.  You *have* to consult deployed.json.

>                 data = os.urandom(size)
> ```

it occurs to me that we don't need to actually provide random data here, we could just do something like `data = 'A'*size`, if we wanted to. this would make this code simpler, and also reduce the ability of the analyzed program to influence the analysis machine (affecting its entropy pool). it would also make using manticore more deterministic; different runs of manticore on the same binary that uses getrandom would behave the same way, which can be nice.

a con would be that manticore would be somewhat less fit for just running programs, but i don't find this particularly compelling.

Part of the broader context here is time zone usage in the library's Python interface. I would suggest the following guidelines for this library:

* Returned `datetime` objects should generally be in UTC. They may be naive or timezone-aware, depending on the particular interface.

* Interfaces may require input `datetime` objects to be naive UTC. It is preferable to also accept timezone-aware `datetime` objects, but in that case, naive `datetime` objects (assumed to be UTC) must also be accepted.

Naive `datetime` objects can be easier to work with, while aware `datetime` objects have the virtue of being self-documenting. There may also be other considerations for particular interfaces. People familiar with the issue sometimes have a preference for working in one style or the other. The Python standard library, of course, defines a framework encompassing both styles but provides no concrete `tzinfo` classes (not even for UTC).

The problem is "it". Wrapping "it" is ill-defined if people are bringing their own transport layer. In some sense we can catch something caused by the low-level `http` library but we really have no gaurantee what that layer will do. Also `BrokenPipeError` is in `__builtins__` in Python 3.0, but not 2.0.

The MR Datastore input source is based on the datastore API which does not support cross name space queries. Indeed, it could have started with a metadata query to get all the name spaces and then for each namespace create a separate query to iterate over but I am not aware of any plans to do that so far. However, doing that in the application level and tying all the individual MR jobs in one pipeline job (using the AE Pipeline API - see this [example](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-mapreduce/blob/master/java/example/src/com/google/appengine/demos/mapreduce/randomcollisions/UsingPipelineServlet.java)) should not be too bad and allow the namespaces to be processed in parallel.

But I don't yet know the exact data ranges that user will want for the calculation, so the fetch job can't trigger the calculation jobs.  Hence I want to start the fetch job, get a `PromisedValue` for its completion, store its handle somewhere, and then use that handle to make my calculation jobs wait until the data fetch is complete before they actually run (or run immediately if it is indeed complete when they're created).

Note: the issue comes from the function being pickled, but the references to the modules in the global namespace do not grab the modules themselves.  Thus, the functions, when unpickled on another process, try to find the modules in the global namespace... and they are not imported.  So, either better pickling is needed, or you can help by making the functions you are shipping self-encapsulated.

Totally agree that we need this feature.  @areitz: It's nice that pants makes an effort with requirements.txt but it's a super heavyweight tool for most people and also it doesn't even really support the spec: https://pip.pypa.io/en/latest/reference/pip_install.html#requirements-file-format

I started working on this about a year ago and pushed a preview branch a few months ago here:
https://github.com/wickman/pex/blob/wickman/requirements_txt/pex/requirements.py

The challenge is that requirements.txt is pretty pip-specific, even letting pip command line flags creep into the specification.  It's also just poorly designed and does not compose particularly well (e.g. with nested requirements.txt files.)  It's also constantly changing, as both pip and setuptools have undergone significant change over the last year.  Writing greenfield code to support the abstractions of requirements.txt in a sensible way has been difficult mostly because it's just not a very sensible spec.

It's my intention that pex 1.0 will support requirements.txt and change the pex command line flags to resemble pip's more closely (e.g. instead of pex -r req1 -r req2, it will be pex -r requirements.txt req1 req2) but it's probably a month away before I can devote the time to finish this up.

We have a working theory, although we haven't run it to ground.  Despite the use of `OrderedSet` in pex, there are some order indeterminate operations.  Specifically, I think the order in which dists are activated is dependent on `PYTHONHASHSEED`.  Couple that with a broken `.pth` file, and if you're unlucky enough to load that broken `.pth` file early, the exception gets swallowed *and* pex stops loading subsequent `.pth` files, thus breaking the later namespace packages.  If you're lucky enough to load the broken `.pth` file later, you can get mostly working namespace packages (although, anything after the broken `.pth` file will not be importable).

Once you find the `PYTHONHASHSEED` value that causes the unfortunate ordering, the problem is 100% reproducible.  I got lucky; my loop searching for `PYTHONHASHSEED` found the failure at 119.  Of course, this was *exceedingly* lucky given how big that environment variable can be.

So I think the ask for pex is twofold:

* Provide much better diagnostics when broken `.pth` files are loaded
* When a broken `.pth` file is loaded, *either* skip it and continue or fail fatally

It seems like the current behavior is the worst of both worlds: the exception in the `.pth` file is swallowed *and* pex stops processing further `.pth` files.

(The fact that we have a broken `.pth` file is of course our problem, but we need better diagnostics and behavior from pex to be able to find that brokenness.)

So scrooge gen fails to add the synthetic java library to and dependees because there are none (your dummy target adds one and that's why this works).  More importantly, the synthetic target it adds to the context does not show up in self.context.targets().  That is a behavior change that came from the big target refactor in may and thus broke ScroogeGen.  If you read the code for add_new_target and targets in Context pre-refactor [1], you'll see the synthetic target would have shown up in self.context.targets() at that point in time.

I encourage less thought and more code reading here.  Thinking is vastly over-rated.

> The underlying design flaw, I think, is that BinaryUtil does two orthogonal things: it computes URLs for requested binaries, and it handles fetching. It might be best to break it up into two separate pieces, along those lines. Then the distinction between "how we get a URL" (which used to only have one answer but now has two) and "how we download" gets clarified.

This makes sense to me -- I was already feeling uneasy about adding yet another argument to the burgeoning argument lists in BinaryUtil. I think it would be feasible to split up the functionality you describe and wrap it together in BinaryUtil to keep up the facade until we can remove it. I think it might make sense as is though, let me know your thoughts:

> However this means that some binary tools use `--binaries-baseurls` and others don't. It's a bit weird to pass urls around as arguments, in a class that also has its own way of finding urls, that may or may not actually be used.

So the `--urls` parameter can default to something that the BinaryTool provides, but I don't think of it as being specific to the tool at all -- if `dist_url_versions` is empty in the BinaryTool class, the tool will fall back to pants binaries hosting, *unless* you provide a value in pants.ini/the command line/whatever for `--urls` in that tool's scope, in which case the tool will be fetched from the urls specified. For instance, if you have some separate url to fetch binaries on your internal network (which may not conform to the pants binaries directory layout at all), you can point any BinaryTool to whatever url you want. So I would say that using `--binaries-baseurls` is something some *classes* do, that's just a good default. If a user wants to fetch these binaries from pants hosting for any reason, they can explicitly set the `--urls` option to `{}` in pants.ini, which will override any default urls that the BinaryTool may have provided. This mode of interaction seems to tie the fetching with the url generation as you described in a way I think makes sense, but I could be missing something.

The formatter already has the knowledge and is normally not stateless. Therefore, if you need this information store it in ``Formatter.step()`` to be accessible in ``Formatter.match()``. The occurs in other cases, too. For example, how do you in which feature you are when scenario is called ?

Example:

```python
class MyFormatter(Formatter):
...
    def step(self, step):
        self.current_step = step
        ...

    def match(self, matchObject):
         do_something_with_step(matchObject, self.current_step)

    def result(self, result):
        ...
        self.current_step = None   # Invalidate step data again.
```

NOTES:
* The matchObject has a reference to the step-function that is matched by a step-definition
* Look at the PrettyFormatter implementation
* Use ``repr(matchObject)`` instead of ``str(matchObject)``
* ``Formatter.step()`` is called before the step is executed
* ``Formatter.result()`` is called after the step is executed and the outcome (result) is known
* ``Formatter.eof()`` marks end-of-feature: See [JsonFormatter.eof()](https://github.com/behave/behave/blob/master/behave/formatter/json.py#L185)
* ``Formatter.close()`` marks end-of-testrun

Otherwise, look at the implementation of other formatters.

> The goal here is to have something that'll work for all the major uses of multi-process instrumentation in Python, with semantics that are as close as we can get them to the threaded equivalent.

I don't feel that this moves away from this goal. It offers an adapter to bring a "built-in" speed and efficiency increase given that users are running their Python WSGI app under one of the main WSGI servers used in production environments. Were Gunicorn to have similar features, it would make sense to optimize for them as well, I feel.

That said, I see now that the uWSGI "adapter" should be opt-in, rather than automatic if `uwsgi` is importable. I can make that change.

The thesis of this PR is just: the tradeoff of the universality of using `shelve` for a large increase in disk hits is unacceptable for my environment. I should run some performance tests to verify the fact that shelve will be slow in this context, but I'm not completely sure that's necessary, unless you want me to do it. `shelve` is slow, and disk is slow.

> Firstly, it's a cache not a datastore so we need to worry about expiry.

The default settings for the uWSGI cache has expiration disabled. Additionally, I think if a user is concerned with the number of cache entries, cache block size, etc., that's a deployment concern for the user that they take on. They should be considering that just as they consider the implications of writing shelve files to disk.

> Secondly it appears to support modes meant for multi-machine synchronisation including UDP and Django's database. If a user is using one of these then the semantics and performance will not be as desired.

It does, but there's no reason to use these features in a Prometheus setup. If you have multiple nodes exposing metrics, it's better to have your Prometheus server scrape multiple nodes than side-aggregating all of the metrics using a uWSGI feature, and expose metrics on a single node. These features are not on by default, and caches can be named and distinct. The user could opt to have one named cache use UDP sync and not another. I will update the PR to support a named cache; that's an oversight.

> Finally it only works for those using uWSGI so even if we can handle the previous issues, we're left with all the other use cases.

Again, I think this should be viewed as an optimization, not a catch-all. For those using uWSGI (I am assuming that's not a small number), it should be much faster and won't hit disk.

> We do need to support floats. As it can only store a 64bit integer and we're using 64bit floats, one approach is to put the float's machine representation in the cache.

This is what I'd like to do. The multiplication thing is a hack.

> At a high level I think this could be an option for some of our users, but it's not a full solution for the multi-process problem.

I spent some time thinking this through before I embarked on the PR. What are the ways other programs cross the process divide? There's a standalone database, shared disk, shared memory, or a shared network resource, more or less. Databases and shared network resources are no good because they implicate a significant dependency for the end user for what should be a lightweight library. Shared disk is well-known, but slow. Shared memory is fast but requires management and allocation.

Arguably, shared disk is one of the few things that comes somewhat universally available in a Python implementation. You don't want to assume you have shared memory across the user's processes because that would be managed "above" Python. So, I think the disk-based route is your baseline, and it serves your broadest audience, unless a shared-memory solution is available (and uWSGI provides this). There's still caveats with disk, even. Google AppEngine's Python runtime doesn't allow writing to disk, for example.

My opinion is that in this scenario, it makes sense to offer additional "adapters" to optimize for a certain environment so that some users can benefit from better performance, but nearly everyone can use it one way or another.

> As it stands the only thing preventing #66 being a full solution is getting a shelve equivalent that support concurrency and that doesn't call fsync on every write to disk.

I don't think I know of one of these that isn't a database and not a persistence utility. You essentially need to buffer before disk, and you want concurrent access to that buffer; there are options here, like locking on the shelve database and flush periodically, but at that point you're looking like a database again and it still has to flush periodically. And, between the flush interval, the exported metrics will be stale.

for what you're trying to do, the tag method would make more sense than the parse method. also, great timing with this question because I just wrote some code to do this task yesterday:

```
import csvkit
import usaddress

# expected format in input.csv: first column 'id', second column 'address'
with open('input.csv', 'rU') as f:
    reader = csvkit.DictReader(f)

    all_rows = []
    for row in reader:
        try:
            parsed_addr = usaddress.tag(row['address'])
            row_dict = parsed_addr[0]
        except:
            row_dict = {'error':'True'}
            
        row_dict['id'] = row['id']
        all_rows.append(row_dict)

field_list = ['id','AddressNumber', 'AddressNumberPrefix', 'AddressNumberSuffix', 'BuildingName', 
              'CornerOf','IntersectionSeparator','LandmarkName','NotAddress','OccupancyType',
              'OccupancyIdentifier','PlaceName','Recipient','StateName','StreetName',
              'StreetNamePreDirectional','StreetNamePreModifier','StreetNamePreType',
              'StreetNamePostDirectional','StreetNamePostModifier','StreetNamePostType',
              'SubaddressIdentifier','SubaddressType','USPSBoxGroupID','USPSBoxGroupType',
              'USPSBoxID','USPSBoxType','ZipCode', 'error']

with open('output.csv', 'wb') as outfile:
    writer = csvkit.DictWriter(outfile, field_list)
    writer.writeheader()
    writer.writerows(all_rows)
```

You dont really need an API for http://xip.io/, you just prefix it with your IP address as illustrated on the page. It is nothing but a wildcard DNS provider that reads the IP encoded in the hostname itself.

> I thought about this before implementing it as well but opted for decoding at time of replay so the true(r) capture is preserved in IPFS instead of one we have further manipulated.

Well, the "true"-rity of the essence of the response is preserved in `Content-Encoding`. In contrast, the `Transfer-Encoding` only deals with how the data is transported between the server and the client; or more precisely, any on-the-fly transformation to the data is done with the `Transfer-Encoding` which needs to be undone on the reception.

I see one logical issue with dechunking though. If the original headers are utilized to understand the payload, the parser might fail as it would expect a chuked encoded data. For now it will be an out-of-band knowledge, unless we know a more expressive way to convey the changes made. One way would be to mask the `Transfer-Encoding` and add a `Content-Length` header, but that would be too much fabrication.

> Your final point is very important: if two HTML pages are the exact same content at different URI-Rs, their hash should be the same. Only the index (i.e., cdxj) will associate the different URIs.

Not just the same content on different URIs, but the same URI might return differently chunked response each time it is requested.

Additional issues discovered:

* The cause of the reported issue is a ChunkedEncodingError, that occurs because the requests library and App Engine don't work so well together by default. See the [work-around](https://toolbelt.readthedocs.io/en/latest/adapters.html#appengineadapter).
* By default, AdWordsClient will use Zeep. In `api_handler.py`, the `APIHandler.__init__` should specify a cache kwarg value of `ZeepServiceProxy.NO_CACHE`.

All of these issues are actually isolated to the example itself; no changes to the client in 11.0.0/1 will be necessary. You *can* work with this version, provided that you make the aforementioned changes. As mentioned earlier, we'll push these changes with the next release.

# What's the use?

**To put things in context:** A typical scenario that one usually faces is - I have my experiment config file working up and running but there are yet many things that can be configured for which there is no obvious choice. Only aparant way is to see empirically what works better and obtain the analysable trends. This PR is first step towards easy explorations of sub-experiments

A typical work flow for such a requirement would be:

1. Generate subexperiment config files and organize them with easily identifiable file names. (`generate-subexperiments`)
2. Train different models for each of the generated sub-experiment config files (`train-subexperiments`)
3. Evaluate these models on one or more evaluation datasets. (`evaluate-subexperiments`)
4. Generate analysable html reports that clearly say what was the change from root-experiment and what was the effect. (`analyse-subexperiments`)

The `generate-subexperiments` commands deals with part (1) of this requirement. It does so by taking in two files: `root_experiment_config` and `generator_config` and outputs all the identifiable sub-experiment config files in some specified `subexperiments_directory`.

The commands for (2), (3) and (4) are not in PR yet. But they basically, consume `subexperiments_directory` and do their required task.

The `generator_config` file gives a **structured and flexible** way to specify what your sub-epxeriments exploration will be. Following section explains it.

# Usage of generator config

For most examples, I will be using venue_classifier.json` from [here](https://github.com/allenai/allennlp-as-a-library-example/blob/master/experiments/venue_classifier.json) as root experiment config.

## Change

The underlying idea of generating **a** sub-experiment from a root experiment is a sequence of `Change`s. These `Change`s will be executed starting from root experiment **sequentially** to finally give use **one** sub-experiment. Something like: (`root_experiment_config` -> `Change1`, `Change2` ... `ChangeN` -> `sub_experiment_config`). So a `sub_experiment` config is nothing but a sequence of changes executed on `root_experiment_config`.

A `Change` simply put encodes which key in current config should be set to which value on execution of this change. It consists of `key_tuple`, `value_tuple`, `key_name` and `value_name`.  `key_tuple` is a **tuple** of keys and `value_tuple` is a **tuple** of values in which individual `key`, `value`'s have following roles.

- A key (str) refers to what "key" of `source_config` one wants to change.
    - Eg. `"model.title_encoder.bidirectional"`, `"trainer.batch_size"` etc.
- A value (str) refers to what "value" one wants to set that key to.
    - Eg. `"true"`, `"10"`, `"[20, 20, 20]"`, `"['relu']"`, `"'path/to/file'"` etc. A value is always a string irrespective of you want the value to be int / list/ dict/ str. See difference between `"true"` and `"'path/to/file'"` (extra `'` in `"'path/to/file'"`). Reason for this will be clear later.
- `key_name` and `value_name` give easy way of identifying the `Change`. Since a supexperiment is a unique sequence of changes, names of constituent changes of subexperiment give easily identifiable name to that sub-experiment.

Here, `key_tuple` and `value_tuple` are of same cardinality and ith key refers to ith value.

### Change (from params): Example 1

Lets say we want to construct `change` for turning Off bidirectionality of seq2vec-encoder for `title_encoder` :
```python
# Change: Make title_encoder unidirectional
{
    "key_tuple": ["model.title_encoder.bidirectional"],
    "value_tuple": ["false"],
    "key_name": "title-enc-bidirectional"
    "value_name": "false",
}
```

When this `change` is `executed` on our `venue_classifier` config, it will return a config file with unidirectional title encoder and all else unchanged. The name of this change will be: `title-enc-bidirectional=false`, which is representative of what it does on execution.

### Change (from params): Example 2

But, actually we have 2 seq2vec encoders in our model. Let's say we want to construct a change for turning bidirectional Off for both of them and identify this change as `all_bidirectional=false`.

```python
# Change: Make title_encoder and abstract_encoder unidirectional
{
    "key_tuple": ["model.title_encoder.bidirectional", "model.abstract_encoder.bidirectional"],
    "value_tuple": ["false", "false"],
    "key_name": "all_bidirectional"
    "value_name": "false",
}
```
See that corresponding entries in key_tuple and value_tuple form the corresponding pairs.
The name of this change will be: `all_bidirectional=false`.

Side note: This abstraction is implemented in `Change` class.

## Sequence of Changes

A sequence of changes is just list (sequence) of changes. For example, we want to make a sequence of changes for (1) make all seq2vec encoders unidirectional and (2) make embeddings trainable, it would be something as following:

```python
# Sequence of following changes:
# (1) making all seq2vec encoders unidirectional
# (2) make embedding trainable
[
{
    "key_tuple": ["model.title_encoder.bidirectional", "model.abstract_encoder.bidirectional"],
    "value_tuple": ["false", "false"],
    "key_name": "all_bidirectional"
    "value_name": "false",
}
{
    "key_tuple": ["model.text_field_embedder.tokens.trainable"],
    "value_tuple": ["true"],
    "key_name": "embeddings_trainable"
    "value_name": "true",
}
]
```

Name of sequence of changes reflects what changes it constitutes and in what order. The name of this sequence of changes will be: `all_bidirectional=false,embeddings_trainable=true`. As one can expect from name: when it will be `execute`d on `root_experiment_config`, it will make all seq2vec encoders unidirectional and make embeddings trainable.

Side note: This abstraction is implemented in `SequenceOfChanges` class.

## Group of Changes

A group of changes are the changes that one wants to take effect one at at time. Eg. lets say I want to try three types of `title_encoder`'s: "lstm", "gru", "rnn", then the `key` is `"model.title_encoder.type"` and values to be explored are `"'lstm'"`, `"'gru'"`, `"'rnn'"`. Clearly, a group will have 1 `key_tuple` and many `value_tuple`s that one wants to explore - one at a time. For this example, following params would construct such a group:

```python
{
    "key_tuple": ["model.title_encoder.type"],
    "value_tuples": [
                 ["lstm"], # value_tuple 1
                 ["gru"],  # value_tuple 2
                 ["rnn"],  # value_tuple 3
              ]
    "key_name": "title_encoder_type"
    "value_names": ["lstm", "gru", "rnn"],
}
```

Side note: This abstraction is implemented in `GroupOfChanges` class.

## Putting things together

Since we know what `Change`, `SequenceOfChanges` and `GroupOfChanges` are, building generator-config file is straight forward. A generator-config file has 3 parts. Following are the type annotations:

```python
{
   "pre_combine_changes": [], # Sequence of Changes
   "combine_changes": []  # List of Group of Changes
   "post_combine_changes": [] # Sequence of Changes
}
```

`pre_combine_changes` and `post_combine_changes` are pre and post combine hooks for executing sequence of changes before and after making combinations. For now, let's skip them - they are not useful for now.

The list of group/s in `"combine_changes"` is central for generating sub-experiments. The generated subexperiments will be combinations from these groups.

### Example 1

Lets say we want to try the combinations of:

1. try *lstm* and *gru* seq2vec encoders
2. try *bidirectional* and *unidirectional* seq2vec encoders


First step is to generate `GroupOfChanges` for (1) and (2).

A group of changes for (1) will be as follows:

```
    # Group 1: X 2 choices
    { # try *lstm* and *gru* seq2vec encoders
    "key_tuple": ["model.title_encoder.type", "model.abstract_encoder.type"],
    "value_tuples": [
                 ["lstm", "lstm"], # value_tuple 1
                 ["gru", "gru"  ], # value_tuple 2
              ]
    "key_name": "encoders_type"
    "value_names": ["lstm", "gru"],
    }
```

And group of changes for (2) will be as follows:

```
    # Group 2: X 2 choices
    { # try *bidirectional* and *unidirectional* seq2vec encoders
    "key_tuple": ["model.title_encoder.bidirectional", "model.abstract_encoder.bidirectional"],
    "value_tuples": [
                 ["true", "true"],     # value_tuple 1
                 ["false", "false"  ], # value_tuple 2
              ]
    "key_name": "all_bidirectional"
    "value_names": ["true", "false"],
    }
```

Finally, we just push these two in `combine_changes` list since we want to make combinations of these groups. Following is the full generator config file for such a generation:

```python
"combine_changes": [
    # Group 1: X 2 choices
    { # try *lstm* and *gru* seq2vec encoders
    "key_tuple": ["model.title_encoder.type", "model.abstract_encoder.type"],
    "value_tuples": [
                 ["lstm", "lstm"], # value_tuple 1
                 ["gru", "gru"  ], # value_tuple 2
              ]
    "key_name": "encoders_type"
    "value_names": ["lstm", "gru"],
    }

    # Group 2: X 2 choices
    { # try *bidirectional* and *unidirectional* seq2vec encoders
    "key_tuple": ["model.title_encoder.bidirectional", "model.abstract_encoder.bidirectional"],
    "value_tuples": [
                 ["true", "true"],     # value_tuple 1
                 ["false", "false"  ], # value_tuple 2
              ]
    "key_name": "all_bidirectional"
    "value_names": ["true", "false"],
    }

]
```

**Pictorially**, it is something like following:

```
                   encoders_type=lstm     all_bidirectional=true
root_experiment -> encoders_type=gru  ->  all_bidirectional=false  -> sub_experiment

                      [ Group 1]               [ Group 2]
                   (choose 1 here)           (choose 1 here)
```

This will generate 4 sub-experiments with following representative names (and as expected behaviour). These names will be present in the file names which these configs will be dumped to.

```
encoders_type=lstm,all_bidirectional=true
encoders_type=lstm,all_bidirectional=false
encoders_type=gru,all_bidirectional=true
encoders_type=gru,all_bidirectional=false
```

More concretely, the following will be the dumped file names:

```
<root_experiment_name>.encoders_type=lstm,all_bidirectional=true.json
<root_experiment_name>.encoders_type=lstm,all_bidirectional=false.json
<root_experiment_name>.encoders_type=gru,all_bidirectional=true.json
<root_experiment_name>.encoders_type=gru,all_bidirectional=false.json
```

This actually makes it very easy to debug, manage and analyse inidividual subexperiments. This is because, now we have an easy handle on individual sub-experiment config files with file-names representative of what is being explored in that particular sub-experiment.

Note that I gave here short example that results in only 4 subexperiments for brevity. Actualy one might want to explore much more (I typically try to analyse 10-15 subexperiments in one time). It's easy to imagine that w/o a structured way of organizing as I show above, sub-experiments exploration can become quite cumbersome.


### Example 2

Using combinations isn't necessary to be used. In some cases, one could just want to try different values. Let's say one wants on only explore all types of sec2vec encoder types and that it.

The following generator config file will do the required:

```python
"combine_changes": [
    # Group 1: X 2 choices
    { # try *lstm*, *gru*, *rnn* seq2vec encoders
    "key_tuple": ["model.title_encoder.type", "model.abstract_encoder.type"],
    "value_tuples": [
                 ["lstm", "lstm", "lstm"], # value_tuple 1
                 ["gru", "gru", "gru"],    # value_tuple 2
                 ["rnn", "rnn", "rnn"],    # value_tuple 3
              ]
    "key_name": "encoders_type"
    "value_names": ["lstm", "gru", "rnn"],
    }
]
```

**Pictorially**, it is something like following:

```
                   encoders_type=lstm
root_experiment -> encoders_type=gru  -> sub_experiment
                   encoders_type=rnn

                      [ Group 1]
                   (choose 1 here)
```


This will generate 3 sub-experiments with following names (and as expected behaviour)
```
encoders_type=lstm
encoders_type=gru
encoders_type=rnn
```

Using this one can simply create different types of sub-experiments with or without combinations. `post_combine_changes` becomes useful with usage of jsonnet as shown in next section. Please note that using jsonnet features is not always required. Extrapolating the above 2 examples, it should be evident that even w/o it, one can do fairly good amont of sub-experiment config files generation.

## Using Jsonnet Features with it

### Without Reference:

The `value` in `Change` is actually any valid jsonnet expression in string. jsonnet expression do not need to yield a json / dict always. It can yield any type of data: string / int / bool / list etc. This makes using or not using jsonnet seamless.

Lets say we know some `model.feedforward` has 3 `num_layers` and we want to set dropout of 0.2 for all the layers. The following `Change` would work.

```python
# Change: Set dropout
{
    "key_tuple": ["model.feedforward"],
    "value_tuple": ["[0.2  for _ in std.range(1, 3)]"],
    "key_name": "dropout"
    "value_name": "0.2",
}
```
Note: value `"[0.2  for _ in std.range(1, 3)]"` is a valid jsonnet expression (try [here](https://jsonnet.org/)) and hence it works.


### With Reference:

In above example, we assumed we knew that `model.feedforward` has 3 `num_layer`s. However, it's possible that it could have been changed before. In such cases, one can use `current` jsonnet variable that refers to the current state of experiment config. For example if a subexperiment is as follows: `root_experiment` -> `Change1` -> `Change2` -> `Change3` -> `sub_experiment` and we use current variable in `Change2`, it refers to the state of experiment config right after `Change1` is executed and before `Change2` is exectued.

For example, if unlike above example, we don't know the num_layers of feedforward upfront / or it's dynamic being changed in previous changes, then following can be used. It assures that dropout setting is consistent with `num_layers` independent of what `num_layers` were set to in previous cases.

```python
# Change: Set dropout
{
    "key_tuple": ["model.feedforward"],
    "value_tuple": ["[0.2  for _ in std.range(1, current.feedforward.num_layers)]"],
    "key_name": "dropout"
    "value_name": "0.2",
}
```

Below is a more concrete example would explain the need of `current` variable.

Lets say I want to explore the following:

1. try model.feedforward.num_layers to 2, 3, 4  [group of 3 changes]
2. try dropouts 0.0, 0.2  [group of 2 changes]

This combination will have 3 X 2 = 6 sub-experiments. Note that value of second group actually depends on what was being set in the first group. `num_layers` are dynamic and can't be known upfront. Leveraging `current.` variable, the following `generator config` solves this problem.

```python
"combine_changes": [
    # Group 1: X 3 choices
    { # model.feedforward.num_layers to 2, 3, 4
    "key_tuple": ["model.feedforward.num_layers"],
    "value_tuples": [
                 ["2"], # value_tuple 1
                 ["3"], # value_tuple 2
                 ["4"], # value_tuple 3
              ]
    "key_name": "ff_num_layers"
    "value_names": ["1", "2", "3"],
    }

    # Group 2: X 2 choices
    { # try dropouts 0.0, 0.2 for feedforward
    "key_tuple": ["model.feedforward.dropouts"],
    "value_tuples": [
                 ["[0.0  for _ in std.range(1, current.feedforward.num_layers)]"],  # value_tuple 1
                 ["[0.2  for _ in std.range(1, current.feedforward.num_layers)]"],  # value_tuple 2
              ]
    "key_name": "all_bidirectional"
    "value_names": ["true", "false"],
    }
]
```

The scenario is something like following:

```
                   ff_num_layers=2     dropouts=0.0
root_experiment -> ff_num_layers=3 ->  dropouts=0.2  -> sub_experiment
                   ff_num_layers=4

                      [ Group 1]        [ Group 2]
                   (choose 1 here)     (choose 1 here)

                                  ^
                                  |

                          `current` var in group 2
                          refers  to  config after
                          choice from group 1  has
                          been made  and  executed

```

Note, the usage of `current.` variable is essential in this case because in that specific sub-experiment `num_layers` cannot be known upfront. The variable `current.` refers to state of experiment config just before the execution of that change in sequence of changes. This allows to inspect any field / key / etc in current state of config and make decisions based on that.

Such a scenario also calls for usage of `post_combine_changes`. Note that even though, we adjust `dropout` according to num_layers set in that sub-experiment, the final config is not yet valid. One needs to make corrections / adjustments to the `activations` and `hidden_sizes` based on what `num_layers` were set to. However, it is just mandatory post correction to make sub-experiment valid, and not something that we are deliberately try to explore. All sub-experiments will need such a post correction, so we add this sequnce in `post_combine_changes` hook. In entirity the generator config file would look like:


```python
{

  # combine_changes is same as above.
  "combine_changes": [
      # Group 1: X 3 choices
      { # model.feedforward.num_layers to 2, 3, 4
      "key_tuple": ["model.feedforward.num_layers"],
      "value_tuples": [
                   ["2"], # value_tuple 1
                   ["3"], # value_tuple 2
                   ["4"], # value_tuple 3
                ]
      "key_name": "ff_num_layers"
      "value_names": ["1", "2", "3"],
      }

      # Group 2: X 2 choices
      { # try dropouts 0.0, 0.2 for feedforward
      "key_tuple": ["model.feedforward.dropouts"],
      "value_tuples": [
                   ["[0.0  for _ in std.range(1, current.feedforward.num_layers)]"],  # value_tuple 1
                   ["[0.2  for _ in std.range(1, current.feedforward.num_layers)]"],  # value_tuple 2
                ]
      "key_name": "all_bidirectional"
      "value_names": ["true", "false"],
      }
  ]

  # Sequnce of Changes for Post Combine Corrections.
  "post_combine_changes": [
    {
      "key_tuple": ["model.feedforward.activations"],
      "value_tuple": ["['relu'  for _ in std.range(1, current.feedforward.num_layers)]"]
    }
    {
      "key_tuple": ["model.text_field_embedder.tokens.trainable"],
      "value_tuple": ["[200  for _ in std.range(1, current.feedforward.num_layers)]"],
    }
  ]

}
```

This will generate the following sub-experiment names:

```
ff_num_layers=2,all_bidirectional=true
ff_num_layers=2,all_bidirectional=false
ff_num_layers=3,all_bidirectional=true
ff_num_layers=3,all_bidirectional=false
ff_num_layers=4,all_bidirectional=true
ff_num_layers=4,all_bidirectional=false
```

Note that post combination changes won't count towards the sub-experiment name since they are common across all sub-experiments.


### Summary of Generator Config:


To **summarize**, a general generator config builds on idea of sequence of Changes and if all sections are used pictorially it would look like:

```
root_experiment --> [ pre-change-1 -> pre-change-2 .. pre-change-N ]   -->  [  Group 1       Group 2    ..  Group 3    ]      -->  [ post-change-1 -> post-change-2 .. post-change-N ]  -->  sub-experiment
                                                                                 .             .             .
                                                                                 .             .             .
                                   "pre_combine_changes"                      choose-1     choose-1    ..  choose-1                        "post_combine_changes"

                                                                                             "combine_changes"
```


# Importance of representative names

From above examples, I would be evident that names of sub-experiments (composed from constituent changes) actually makes it easily managable. Having viewable sub-experiment config files with relevant names makes it easy to verify that sub-experiment is as expected as file-name suggests. However, that's not only benefit:

Although `report_experiments` command is not PR-ready, the final report makes a lot of sense if you have given names to individual changes that you are exploring.  Eg. If there are 4 subexperiments being explored:
```
all_bidirectional=true,embedding_trainable=true
all_bidirectional=true,embedding_trainable=false
all_bidirectional=false,embedding_trainable=true
all_bidirectional=false,embedding_trainable=false
```
it would generate searchable html report for each configured evauation dataset as (check #1566 for sample example):

| all_bidirectional|embedding_trainable|metric_1|metric_2|
|----------|:-------------:|------:|------:|
| true |  true    | 0.8 | 0.6
| true |  false   | 0.3 | 0.7
| false | true    | 0.6 | 0.3
| false | false   | 0.7 | 0.5

The ability to club changes in one logical name also helps analysis. They abstract out internal details of what that change constitutes. Eg. `all_bidirectional` actually means bidirectional of both abstract and title encoder but it's a redundant detail for analysis and organizing the sub-experiment file names.


# Other Comments

> what problem does this solve that jsonnet doesn't already solve?

I hope it's clear from these examples that the purposes are fundamentally different. jsonnet features are addon to sub-experiment generation command and one can handle fairly good scenarios of sub-epxeriment generation even without jsonnet features. I specifically delayed using jsonnet in examples to clarify this point.

> Why not just bake all of this into your jsonnet ... and iterate over combinations of environment variables?

The 3 reasons I gave in above comment and examples here support why not. Lastly, abstractions that `Change`, `SequenceOfChanges` and `GroupOfChanges` give to generate subexperiments from root experiment appears much cleaner to me and are surely more flexible.

> "I read the code multiple times and I'm still not 100% sure what it's doing or how to use it"

I was mistaken that 1 example in original PR and test cases would be sufficient to explain purpose and syntax! But after following examples in **this** comment, **using** generator-config should seem very straight-forward. For reviewing implementation, looking at `Change`, `SequenceOfChanges` and `GroupOfChanges` classes and comparing with their utility from examples above should suffice.

> I don't think anyone would actually be able to use this functionality unless you showed them how ..

I can make a tutorial out of this comment, if it seems appropriate.

> ...  but it's also possible that it's more complicated than it needs to be.

The examples I give above are still simple enough to make a point of each functionality and explain the short pipeline. For very small / simple examples (**implementation wise**) it might seem that a structured approach to sub-experiment generation is an overkill. But I would request to consider the possibility that one might need to handle 10's of sub-experiments explorations and need a way to handle/manage and analyse them. As far as usage / end-user friendliness is concerned, I hold that usage should be quite straightforward for small / big examples after following this comment.

ok, here's the issue:

your dataset_reader needs a `from_params` method, something like

```python
    @classmethod
    def from_params(cls, params: Params) -> 'TeiTextDatasetReader':
        token_indexers = TokenIndexer.dict_from_params(params.pop('token_indexers', {}))
        lazy = params.pop('lazy', False)
        return TEITextDatasetReader(token_indexers=token_indexers, lazy=lazy)
```

I believe that will fix it.

*longer explanation*

the way classes get instantiated from configuration is that the base class from_params pops off the `type` parameter and uses it to delegate to a subclass implementation.

here, `DatasetReader.from_params` pops off `type`, sees `tei_text` and (in essence) calls `TEITextDatasetReader.from_params` with the remaining params. But `TEITextDatasetReader` doesn't define `from_params`, so it drops back to the base class method, which tries to pop off `type` *again*, which it can't, since it was already popped off.

closing, let me know if this doesn't fix your issue, but I think it should.

I think it's great that you can set it on the fly, but I believe that feature should be implemented in the init (which we loosely call `bin/boot`) rather than in publisher itself. The init system can read from etcd for changes and reboot the server gracefully. This will still allow us to change config on the fly, but ties in publisher a little less with etcd. See https://github.com/deis/deis/issues/2701 and https://github.com/deis/deis/blob/master/publisher/main.go

The rationale behind this thinking is that I believe the server itself should be *very* simple. Reflecting on pre-1.0 we generated a lot of technical debt by having these hard ties with third-party projects like etcd. We cannot reliably write unit tests against these components which makes features such as this one mandate a manual test-by-hand, so personally I'd like to refactor these out in the future for better test coverage.

For the non-solver-related use-cases, it's actually already done.

------

#### A digression

I'm going to have people arguing my ear off for this probably, but you'll soon be able to pip install conda.  With two **big** ✳️ asterisks.

✳️  The pip-installed version posted on PyPI will be useful for a python library only.  It will not create entry points.  It will not be useable as most people would normally use conda--as an application.

We will hopefully soon have other methods for installing/bootstrapping conda, including rpm and deb packages.  Maybe even a homebrew recipe.  But pip with all python tooling including wheels in their current form is not capable of installing conda as an application.

✳️ Use conda as a python library at your own risk.  Explicitly pin dependencies for your application, and upgrade diligently but deliberately.  All of conda's internal code *is not* a public API, even though it's written in python, and python doesn't support "private" code.

The single long-term supported API is the command line API.  I'm soon going to add some code that let's you easily use that command line API in process, without having to shell out.  The other "LTS"-ish supported interface is `conda/exports.py`, which conda-build is using.

Digression over.

---------

For the user-requested items you're looking for, try this

```
from pprint import pprint
from conda.history import History

prefix="/some/conda/path/prefix"

history = History(prefix)
with History(prefix) as h:
    pprint(h.get_user_requests())
```

@kalefranz @groutr @msarahan Hmm. Ryan made a good point today. For _soft_ linking, the current code causes a problem. Specifically, consider this scenario:

- Environment 1 installs A-1.0-0.tar.bz2, which is extracted into `pkgs/A-1.0-0`. 
- Environment 2 installs A-1.0-0.tar.bz2 from a *different* channel. Because of the collision, `pkgs/A-1.0-0` must be removed, and replaced with the new package.

If the linking method above is inst.LINK_COPY or inst.LINK_HARD, then this is just fine. But if the linking method in the first case is inst.LINK_SOFT, then we have a problem, because now Environment 1 is either broken, or its contents have been silently replaced. 

This suggests that we cannot, in fact, remove extracted directories from the cache if soft linking is ever employed. Now to be clear, this will not be a problem _unless_ soft linking is employed _and_ there is a channel collision, so most users won't see this, but we should probably decide on a solution. 

As we discussed earlier, the goal of this pull request was to work within the constraints of `conda`'s current cache and metadata structure. This suggests that we're going to have to modify it just a little bit.

OK, I might have been wrong in what I said on the phone the other day. 

When building the repodata index, you have to give priority to the fetch indexes over _both_ the cached packages _and_ the installed packages.

For the installed packages, the relevant logic is [here](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.1.x/conda/api.py#L10-L45). As you can see, the installed packages are iterated through _after_ the repodata has been fetched. However, the local conda-meta information is used _only_ if it is absent from the index. Thus the only time the local conda-meta data is considered for solving purposes is if the package has been removed from the source channel. (One could argue that in that case, we should encourage the replacement of such packages in a user's environment, because their removal from the channel suggests that they are broken or superceded).

For cached packages, the relevant logic is [here](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.1.x/conda/fetch.py#L222-L256). This code is called by `fetch_index`. In [this line](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/4.1.x/conda/fetch.py#L222-L256), we see that the cached metadata is skipped completely if it has already been fetched from a channel. So the cached package metadata is used _only_ if it is not in one of the channels fetched from the internet (e.g., in offline mode).

This was by design when the feature was originally implemented, I believe for the following reasons:
- It mirrors how Python works.  If you say ``x = 'foo'`` then ``x`` will be a string, but if you say ``x = 'foo',`` then x will be a tuple.
- In general, explicit is better than implicit.  If someone defines a value a particular way in their config file, I think the original implementors might have considered it bad form to silently coerce it into a different data type.

With that being said, I think I personally agree with this request to do the automatic coercion, for a few reasons:
- We already do type coercion in other cases.  For example, if you define ``foo = integer`` in your specfile and then say ``foo = "5"`` then ``foo`` will be converted to an integer.  Converting to a list doesn't seem any different from that.
- As @Koris points out, this is confusing to newcomers.  I've definitely seem people bitten by it at my job, where the people editing the config files might not know Python, so the whole "trailing comma makes a tuple" logic is foreign to them and not familiar at all.
- This change would be backwards-compatible in the sense that no currently-valid config file would behave any differently after this change.

I don't know when I'll personally have the time to do any real work on ConfigObj next, but I'd definitely be open to a Pull Request implementing this feature.

I did a bit more benchmarking with dask as well, to (a) check that dask works with bcolz + c-blosc 1.7.0 and no further changes, which it should as c-blosc 1.7.0 still has the same non-contextual global locking functions as before, and bcolz is holding the GIL, and (b) to establish a performance baseline which we might hope to improve by switching bcolz to use the contextual non-locking blosc functions and also releasing the GIL. Results here: http://nbviewer.ipython.org/gist/alimanfoo/88a937ef0953f4a3c5d1/bench_bcolz_blosc_dask.ipynb

I compared three implementations of sum() over a large-ish array: bcolz's carray.sum(), the non-parallel chunked implementation of asum() from the [scikit-allel chunked module](http://scikit-allel.readthedocs.org/en/latest/chunked.html#allel.chunked.core.asum), and dask.array.sum().

I also tried this with two different arrays of data: the first an arange, the second some real genotype data.

Good news is that dask seems to work fine with bcolz + c-blosc 1.7.0 and no further changes. Also, performance is similar between c-blosc 1.4.1 and c-blosc 1.7.0.

The one slightly odd thing is that dask seems pathologically slow when computing a sum over the arange data, although it competes much better when working on real genotype data.

E.g., limiting blosc to 1 thread, for the arange data carray.sum() takes 1.37s, allel.chunked.asum() takes 2.24s, and da.sum() takes at least 4.17s even when using 4 workers.

However, for the genotype array, carray.sum() takes 1.93s, allel.chunked.asum() takes 2.58s, and da.sum() takes 2.12s with 4 workers.

I can't think why the arange data would hurt dask performance specifically, but not the genotype data.

We're about to start testing this workaround:

```python
nthreads = bcolz.set_nthreads(1)
os.fork()
# ... parent process gracefully exits, child continues...
bcolz.set_nthreads(nthreads)
```

Will keep this thread updated with our results.

What does BLOSC_NOLOCK do? I'd assume that it uses a lockless queue implementation instead of OS locks. How would that improve this situation? Our problem is that the fork child doesn't keep the parent processes threads and blosc/bcolz doesn't realize the threads are gone. Changing to a lockless implementation would just mean we deadlock at 100% CPU instead of 0% waiting for threads to respond, wouldn't it? Or does BLOSC_NOLOCK do something else?

In the end I am thinking of a small plugin framework, similar to `bcftools plugin` but then for Python written plugins.

```python
from pysam import plugin  # Or whatever :-)

class MyPlugin(plugin.VariantPlugin):
    """This is my awesome VCF plugin. It doesn't do anything special though,
    just passing the input."""

    @classmethod
    def about(cls):
        return cls.__doc__

    @classmethod
    def usage(cls):
        return "Usage: python MyPlugin.py [General Options]"

    def __init__(self, argv):
        pass

    def parse_args(self, argv):
        pass

    def init(self, vcf_in, vcf_out):
        pass

    def process(self, record):
        return record

    def destroy(self):
        pass

if __name__ == "__main__":
    import sys
    instance = MyPlugin()
    sys.exit(instance.main())
```
On the command line:
```bash
> python MyPlugin.py --regions Chr.2 SampleY.bcf > result.vcf
# or
> bcftools norm -Ou --multiallelics -any SampleY.bcf | python MyPlugin.py > result.vcf
```
Where `--regions Chr.2` and `SampleY.bcf` are passed to a `bcftools view` child process.

The nested transaction scheme was chosen for compatibility with the sqlalchemy ORM: it knows about rolling back to a savepoint and can restore its in-memory objects to their former state. It would be simpler to get rid of it and do what the Go version does, but I think that would have unexpected results (although this isn't tested nearly as well as it ought to be).

Even if the `__exit__` method got smarter about retryable vs non-retryable errors, the rollback could still fail due to network errors, etc. I'm not sure what is the right way to handle errors in rollback. Maybe we need a server-side change to allow ROLLBACK TO SAVEPOINT in more situations.

Apologies, but the Kibana filter is exclude-only, since it filters multiple kinds of indices.  You can tell it `exclude: False`, but it won't do any different:

```
        for index in self.working_list():
            if index in [
                    '.kibana', '.marvel-kibana', 'kibana-int', '.marvel-es-data'
                ]:
                self.__excludify(True, exclude, index)
```

It is a hold-over from Curator 3.  It's exclusively there to prevent you from accidentally deleting `.kibana` indices.  I should probably document this functionality better.

That said, what you want to do can be achieved like this:

```
actions:
  1:
    action: snapshot
    description: Backup the .kibana index
    options:
      ignore_empty_list: True
      repository: YOUR_REPO_NAME
      name: 'kibana-%Y%m%d%H%M%S'
    filters:
    - filtertype: pattern
      kind: regex
      value: '^\.kibana$'
```

You could also keep the `prefix` filtertype you're using now, but you need to escape the `.`, as all of the pattern types are actually part of regular expressions:

This works:
```
    prefix: '\.kibana'
```

It also works with `curator_cli`, but you have to escape the back-slash (rather than the period) in order for it to appear in the resulting JSON:

BAD:
```
$ curator_cli show_indices --filter_list '{"filtertype":"pattern", "kind":"prefix", "value":"\.kibana"}'
Usage: curator_cli show_indices [OPTIONS]

Error: Invalid value for "--filter_list": Invalid JSON: {"filtertype":"pattern", "kind":"prefix", "value":"\.kibana"}
```

GOOD:
```
$ curator_cli show_indices --filter_list '{"filtertype":"pattern", "kind":"prefix", "value":"\\.kibana"}'
.kibana
```

Hi @smoser you are absolutely correct. The managed disk for your previous OS is not deleted when you delete the VM.  Some options:

1) Delete the resource group, which will delete all associated resources.
2) Run the VM create and specify --attach-os-disk (with that disk name) instead of --image, since your OS disk is already set up, this should speed things up.
3) Specify a different name with --os-disk-name. The automatically calculated one is deterministic (by design).

    be used to continually extend the invisibility of a  queue

    role calls get_messages  and recognizes that it needs more time to

On 05/10/2017 05:02 AM, John Paul Adrian Glaubitz wrote:
> I just figured out that the SDK releases are available as single
> tarballs generated from the git tags, they follow this pattern:
> 
> |https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/archive/v?(\d.*)[A-Z,a-z,0-9]*\.zip|
> 
> e.g.:
> 
> |https://github.com/Azure/azure-sdk-for-python/archive/v2.0.0rc6.tar.gz|
> 
> So, I suggest just pulling the tarball from there and using this as a
> base for the packaging.

That doesn't work because the azure-cli releases depend on the
individual components of the SDK, not on the SDK as a whole.

So as packager there are two choices:

a.) Create 1 package for SDK, as we pretty much do in openSUSE right now
and then have a very long list of Provides: statements where each
Provides lists a component. This list is going to be a PITA to maintain
and will inevitably be wrong and cause headachs
b.) Package each individual component of the SDK, the approach we are
now taking.

Usually, the package are at the Resource Provider (RP) level. Example, the "Microsoft.Compute" provider is inside the "azure-mgmt-compute" package. For [Function](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/rest/api/appservice/webapps/getfunction), they are under the "Microsoft.Web" RP, and then in the package "azure-mgmt-web".
If you need a programmatic way to know from a RP what is the package to use, we don't have that :/. Naming is pretty consistent and in 95%, that's really just the RP name.

Thanks for sharing. The issue you are pointing out is not surprising, but due to local penetration depth recovery: the height-field is a group of elements (extruded triangles, forming some kind of 3d prism). If a box smaller than the element width drop in-between multiple elements, those elements push the box sideways instead of upwards. 

We could use triangles instead of extruded triangles (prisms/boxes) elements to avoid this issue.

I think the default log handler writes messages to the stderr stream. We display stderr output with a red background to distinguish it from stdout - though this is a questionable decision,  since output on stderr does not necessarily mean there's a problem.

There are a couple of ways you could distinguish different log levels:

- Use a logging formatter to embed ANSI colour codes into the text (Tornado has such a formatter, for example), and configure the StreamHandler to write to stdout instead of stderr (avoiding the red background).
- Write a custom [logging handler](https://docs.python.org/3/library/logging.html#handler-objects) which sends Jupyter [display_data](http://jupyter-client.readthedocs.io/en/latest/messaging.html#display-data) messages to display logging information as HTML, which gives much more control of how it appears. This would be a bit more work, but it would be an interesting contribution to the ecosystem; it could be released as a standalone package for people to use.

Yes, traits in config are singletons, only class config is merged, so if you have:

```python
c.App.trait = {'a': 1}
```

at one config level, and

```python
c.App.trait = {'b': 2}
```

at another, the assignment at the later point takes precedent, and the result is the second assignment `{'b': 2}`, not the merge of the two.

The best way to think about traitlets config loading is a series of consecutive assignments.

If dict traits were always merged, there would be no way to specify that a value in higher-level config should not be there. Extensions work because the values are bools, and having a False value is equivalent to having removed that value. That's not true of dicts in general.

A notebook has only one language associated with it, so it is generally assumed that setting the language of a notebook should only happen once or twice, not continuously. If you keep switching back and forth, that means that at any given time, much of your notebook is invalid, since the notebook itself is associated with a single language. I know there are people who would like multi-language notebooks, where the notebook can associated different cells with different languages ([Beaker](http://beakernotebook.com), which is based on Jupyter, adds this feature), but this is not the model Jupyter has at the moment. We have been discussing if/how/when we should be adding it, though.

Right now, your best bet is probably to use rpy2 and its `%%R` magic to use Python and R at the same time, or to keep separate R and Python notebooks. The nice thing about `%%R` is that it lets you easily move data back and forth between the two interpreters.

> evaluating the JVP requires about twice the max memory as evaluating the function

This is true for all functions, including those which happen to be grad(something).

I'm pretty sure you can't get around this fact, even in the special case of hvps.

Here's some graphs. Firstly a forward mode computation graph:
```
jvp(fun)  u_1 --> u_2 --> v
           ^       ^      ^
           |       |      |
fun       x_1 --> x_2 --> y
```
and reverse mode:
```
vjp(fun)  u_1 <-- u_2 <-- v
           ^       ^      ^
           |       |      |
fun       x_1 --> x_2 --> y
```

And this is what the hessian vector product graph looks like (I've dropped the subscripts because I'm lazy).
```
jvp(vjp)(fun)  z  <--  z  <--  z 
             ↗︎ ↑     ↗︎ ↑     ↗︎ ^
vjp(fun)   u  <--  u  <--  v   |
           ^   |   ^   |   ^   |
           |   w - | → w - | → w
           | ↗︎     | ↗︎     | ↗︎
fun        x  -->  x  -->  y
```
This graph has the same structure as the vjp one, and you need to record the bottom rows in order to compute the top two rows just like in a vjp.

The information about 32-bit DLLs possibly mucking up the works gave me the thought to check the DLL's in Dependency Walker and I found the problem, fresh Windows 7 installs are missing msvcr100.dll the 64 bit py libraries like libewf, libbde, etc need. tmsh modify asm policy fake_policy active

tmsh publish asm policy fake_policy

> The real problem is that the timezone localization that pytz calculates and attaches via the localize method is calculated only at the beginning and nothing is triggering its recalculation (as compared with the tzfile, which calculates the timezone upon request rather than on creation of the date).

Thanks for the clarification, that makes a lot of sense.  

For what it's worth, btw, I've found dateutil super easy to use, and really powerful.  Unfortunately, django [relies pretty heavily on pytz](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.7/topics/i18n/timezones/) -- so these sorts of behaviors/quirks are probably on me for trying to play mix and match with pytz, dateutil and [icalendar](https://github.com/collective/icalendar) :laughing:

Regarding the tests: They worked because the `tzrange.__eq__` only checks for:

- _std_abbr
- _dst_abbr 
- _std_offset
-  _dst_offset
- _start_delta
- _end_delta

The majority of those are **default** initialized in the parser, so the parser was ignoring the majority of the string and default initializing those. The "worked" but the string used to create the objects was not used in its majority. Therefore this format is not being correctly parsed. As a corollary, no mater what do you write in this format, the result will be the same. Example:

```python

>>> import dateutil.tz
>>> s = "EST5EDT,4,1,0,7200,10,-1,0,7200,3600"
>>> s2 = "EST5EDT,4,4,3,7200,11,-1,0,7200,3600"
>>> tz1 = dateutil.tz.tzstr(s)
>>> tz2 = dateutil.tz.tzstr(s2)
>>> tz1.__dict__
{'_s': 'EST5EDT,4,1,0,7200,10,-1,0,7200,3600', '_std_abbr': 'EST', '_dst_abbr': 'EDT', '_std_offset': datetime.timedelta(-1, 68400), '_dst_offset': datetime.timedelta(-1, 72000), '_start_delta': relativedelta(hours=+2, month=4, day=1, weekday=SU(+1)), '_end_delta': relativedelta(hours=+1, month=10, day=31, weekday=SU(-1)), '_dst_base_offset_': datetime.timedelta(0, 3600), 'hasdst': True}
>>> tz2.__dict__
{'_s': 'EST5EDT,4,4,3,7200,11,-1,0,7200,3600', '_std_abbr': 'EST', '_dst_abbr': 'EDT', '_std_offset': datetime.timedelta(-1, 68400), '_dst_offset': datetime.timedelta(-1, 72000), '_start_delta': relativedelta(hours=+2, month=4, day=1, weekday=SU(+1)), '_end_delta': relativedelta(hours=+1, month=10, day=31, weekday=SU(-1)), '_dst_base_offset_': datetime.timedelta(0, 3600), 'hasdst': True}
```

Notice that both generate `relativedelta(hours=+2, month=4, day=1, weekday=SU(+1))` and `relativedelta(hours=+1, month=10, day=31, weekday=SU(-1))` as `start_delta` and `end_delta`. So if this format was supported at some point, it seems incorrectly parsed,

I am not sure what exactly the issue is here.  It is a feature of PEP 495 that two datetime instances that compare equal may produce different timestamps.  Simplifying the original example,

```python
>>> t = datetime.datetime(2016, 11, 6, 1, 12, 15)
>>> t == t.replace(fold=1)
True
>>> t.timestamp() == t.replace(fold=1).timestamp()
False
```

which finally leads to a failure of installation.

Nevermind, I found a solution and I tested that gist. It was needed just a little fix with converting float variable (AppleScript returns "float" with comma instead of dot, so I just replaced "," to "." and converted to float). This is fixed script, just add it to `/powerline/segments/common/players.py`.

```
class iTunesPlayerSegment(PlayerSegment):
	def get_player_status(self, pl):
		status_delimiter = '-~`/='
		ascript = '''
			tell application "System Events"
				set process_list to (name of every process)
			end tell
			if process_list contains "iTunes" then
				tell application "iTunes"
					if player state is playing then
						set t_title to name of current track
						set t_artist to artist of current track
						set t_album to album of current track
						set t_duration to duration of current track
						set t_elapsed to player position
						set t_state to player state
						return t_title & "{0}" & t_artist & "{0}" & t_album & "{0}" & t_elapsed & "{0}" & t_duration & "{0}" & t_state
					end if
				end tell
			end if
		'''.format(status_delimiter)
		now_playing = asrun(pl, ascript)
		if not now_playing:
			return
		now_playing = now_playing.split(status_delimiter)
		if len(now_playing) != 6:
			return

		now_playing[4] = now_playing[4].replace(',', '.')
		title, artist = now_playing[0], now_playing[1]
		title = title[0:25] + "…" if len(title) > 25 else title
		artist = artist[0:15] + "…" if len(artist) > 15 else artist
		state = _convert_state(now_playing[5])
		total = _convert_seconds(now_playing[4])
		elapsed = _convert_seconds(float(now_playing[4]) - float(now_playing[4]))
		return {
			'title': title,
			'artist': now_playing[1],
			'album': now_playing[2],
			'total': total,
			'elapsed': elapsed,
			'state': state
		}

itunes = with_docstring(iTunesPlayerSegment(),
('''Return iTunes now playing information.
Requires ``osascript``.
{0}
''').format(_common_args.format('itunes')))
```

Hold this. It is a bit more complicated than I first thought.

It is not only the about the `None` case but about all wrong data types.

Current approach is to iterate on fields and get keys from input data. If data has no get method, then `AttributeError` is caught and an error is stored.

There are a few issues with this:

- When `unknown != EXCLUDE`, the code that handles unknown keys fails as `data` is not iterable and `set(data)` won't work. This is what brought me here in the first place.
- The error message for this is the "type" error message from the first field that triggers this, while this is a schema error. It does not really make sense IMHO to pick a random `Field` error message for a schema error.
- If the schema has no field, no error is triggered. A schema without field might not be an obvious use case, but why not? It could make sense when used with `unknown=INCLUDE`, or it could be a corner case of dynamically creating `Schema`s.

I added two commits showing two different approaches to this.

The first catches the `TypeError` when dealing with unknown fields and skips the treatment. It looks awkward and redundant. And it only addresses the first issue in the list above.

The second checks the type of `data` before doing anything. It adds a check to the normal execution path rather than relying solely on try/catch, which is not ideal in terms of performance, but not _that_ bad. Also, it works with `AttributeError`. If `data` has a `get` method but is not an iterable, it is bound to fail. This might not be an issue in practice. I mean it will only break if you try hard. And the `'Invalid input type'` error message is hardcoded.

This reveals underlying design issues. It is not the simple change I thought. (And I came there while trying to tackle https://github.com/marshmallow-code/marshmallow/issues/851...)

Thoughts welcome.

A deleted DNS record will not be updated (as expected), i.e. the following will not work as expected:

* deploy old stack version A with old Senza (no CF update)
* deploy new stack version B with old Senza (no CF update)
* redirect traffic to new stack version B with old Senza (no CF update, this will delete the DNS record for stack version A)
* deploy another stack version C with new Senza (CF update)
* redirect traffic to stack version C with new Senza (CF update, DNS record for A still does not exist)
* now try to redirect traffic back to old stack version A with new Senza

=> stack version A will not get traffic as the new Senza does not create a new DNS record for stack version a (it only tries CF update and `UPSERT` on existing records)

IMHO we don't need to fix this as creating a new DNS record from scratch is too much extra code for this "corner" case.

Yes. We can use for example use push/pop via the API and this results in appropriate SMT2 statements in the log.

For SMTInterpol, you can see the implementation in [LoggingScript](https://github.com/ultimate-pa/smtinterpol/blob/717fe652dd6388bdefc0bca959ea4ed89def1e71/Library-SMTLIB/src/de/uni_freiburg/informatik/ultimate/logic/LoggingScript.java) (their API is just a way to use SMT2 statements via method calls instead of passing strings, so it is very easy for them).

For MathSAT5, you can for example easily try the feature out if you take their [API example](http://mathsat.fbk.eu/apiexample.html), add ```msat_set_option(cfg, "debug.api_call_trace", "1"); msat_set_option(cfg, "debug.api_call_trace_filename", "example.smt");``` directly after one of the `msat_create_config();` calls and build and run the example as explained at its top.
[Here](https://github.com/Z3Prover/z3/files/697003/example1.txt) is the result of doing this for the trivial incremental `example1` function in this program.

If you can produce C log files, that could also be nice (each of the formats has its advantages, of course, so having several of them can be nice for debugging), especially if the C program would only use the public API and thus would be independent of internal changes.
Princess for example also can produce logs as Scala programs using their API in addition to the SMT2 log.

Because I do not know the internals of Z3 I do not know how intermediate expressions would affect the search. But the SMT2 log would be valuable even if it does not guarantee that the search of the SMT solver happens exactly as it did when the API was used. This is also not guaranteed by the other SMT solvers' logs AFAIK. However, in my experience the majority of bugs in SMT solvers I encountered were reproducible with the SMT2 log. So even having an best-effort SMT2 log in addition to the replay log would be nice.

No the idea isn't to remove any code duplication. Can we just clarify what code you think is duplicated here?

As explained earlier by code duplication I'm assuming you mean the

* ``mk_consts_files.py``
* ``mk_def_file.py``
* ``mk_gparams_register_modules_cpp.py``
* ``mk_install_tactic_cpp.py``
* ``mk_mem_initializer_cpp.py``
* ``mk_pat_db.py``
* ``pyg2hpp.py``

scripts? These scripts just call into code inside ``mk_util.py``, they don't duplicate any code in ``mk_util.py``. These scripts provide a convenient command line interface to generating various files which the CMake build system uses to generate the files when necessary. Each script has a tiny bit of boiler plate to add a command line parser and then call code inside ``mk_util`` to do the work. There is a tiny bit of code duplication here but given how simple the ``main()`` functions are I think it's justifiable.

So at least from my perspective I haven't added any unnecessary code duplication. Those scripts need to exist so CMake (and other clients) have a way of generating various files from the command line.

What I'm proposing is to move the functions

* ``mk_pat_db_internal()``
* ``mk_install_tactic_cpp_internal()``
* ``mk_mem_initializer_cpp_internal()``
* ``mk_gparams_register_modules_internal()``
* ``mk_def_file_internal()``
* ``mk_z3const_py_internal()``

and data structures they depend on into their own file and decouple them from using anything in ``mk_util.py``. The functions in ``mk_util`` that called the moved function will simply call them by importing this new file as a module.

The idea is to basically keep code that is used (directly and indirectly via the previously mentioned script) by both build systems separate from code that is only used by the Python build system.

The advantage is that then developers know when they are touching code that effects both build system or code that just effects the python build system.

This is quite minor so I don't particularly strongly about doing this but it was just an idea I had.

Regarding performance: Z3 will try to use a solver that doesn't support incremental queries when there is only one `(check-sat)` command. When it sees the second `(check-sat)`, it will automatically switch to a solver that supports those features, but which is usually slower (in this case likely because the incremental SAT solver does not implement many features that in a non-incremental setting improve performance). So, this is expected, but it may change in the future when we get the fast SAT solver to do incrementality without a big performance penalty).

This is pushing the Datalog engine into a territory where it isn't suited. It takes flat relations and relies on the caller to flatten the encoding. The back-end engines are all table based. So they operate on a notion of relations over finite _enumeration_ domains. Algebraic datatypes allow finite types by nesting, but a representations as tables require an encoding that the backend does not address. 
Besides this, the error message that gets reported has to do with selector functions. These are uninterpreted when applied to constructors that don't match. For example car(nil) does not have a fixed interpretation. Similarly from(nil) and to(nil) do not. Even though the context enforces the variable to be an edge the sanity checking does not take this into account (it does not inspect the context constraints when rejecting the sub-term).
