I didn't have time to do a thorough review, but from what I see, this looks OK to me. I don't have a problem with concentrating fundamental or common schemas in a `schemas` module. (In Haskell, sometimes projects will have a `Types.hs` module where all the models are defined but most of the operations they support omitted. This seems like that to me.) On the other hand, I like better how you put e.g. `VersionResponseSchema` in the same module as the view that serves versions -- it's closer, so it seems to me like it will be more likely to be modified at the same time. So I would say that we should try to do that as much as we can, and fall back to putting things in `schemas` if we have to. At first I thought you put things in `schemas` to re-use them, but I see that e.g. `CollectionRequestSchema` is only used once?

I'm extracting the "both deleted and records" bug as #1396.

I believe what's happening in this one is something like the following:

- The record in question (the default bucket) is deleted at the top of the request.
- Request 1 and request 2 both start around the same time.
- Both requests do `self.get()` to check if the request exists already. It doesn't.
- Both try to delete the tombstone and insert into `records`. One succeeds, the other gets an integrity error.

I don't believe #1386 will fix this one -- we need to stop doing explicit checks for the record using `self.get()`, because that's a classic TOCTOU error.

As a sum-up, hosts and services **Overall status** is computed as described hereunder.

For a service, the overall state is (prioritized):
- a service critical or unreachable (4 - critical)
- a service warning or unknown (3 - warning)
- a service downtimed (2 - in_downtime)
- a service acknowledged (1 - acknowledged)
- a service ok (0 - ok)

For an host, the overall state is (prioritized):
- an host down (4 - critical)
- an host unreachable (3 - warning)
- an host downtimed (2 - in_downtime)
- an host acknowledged (1 - acknowledged)
- an host up (0 - ok)

The overall state of an host is:
- 0 if the host is UP and all its services are OK
- 1 if the host is DOWN or UNREACHABLE and acknowledged or at least one of its services is acknowledged and no other services are WARNING or CRITICAL
- 2 if the host is DOWN or UNREACHABLE and in a scheduled downtime or at least one of its services is in a scheduled downtime and no other services are WARNING or CRITICAL
- 3 if the host is UNREACHABLE or at least one of its services is WARNING
- 4 if the host is DOWN or at least one of its services is CRITICAL

Hi @PipgZYP 
If you are upgrading from older version, please check that `BUILD_WITH_OPENMP` CMake flag is disabled. 
When it is off, the overall CPU utilization should not be reaching 100%. 
There are several CPU intensive operations that we are gradually optimizing:
1. YUY to RGB conversion when using the color sensor
2. Pointcloud / Align methods that require aligning images from one viewport to another
3. Depth colorization (purely aesthetic)  
Also some examples (like `rs-measure` and some of the CV examples) add some work on top of that. 
That said, features that you don't need will not contribute to the CPU utilization - if you just enable HD depth streaming via pipeline the CPU utilization is expected to be reasonably low (8-9% on a modern PC). As you start adding more streams and processing steps, each will increase the total utilization

- requests to v3/repositories/repo-does-not-exist/importers/file/ return 404s
- POST to v3/repositories/my-repo/importers/file/ some=params should return a 200 and set the repository field to my-repo
- the repository field cannot be updated. (an importer cannot switch repos)
- PUT/PATCH/DELETE should return all return 202s

it is more that PPO1 is not gpu-optimized :) PPO1 uses 1 environment per MPI worker. In other words, when you run PPO1 with multiple MPI processes, each process creates its own copy of the environment, and its own neural net (NN). The gradients for NN updates are aggregated across the workers by virtue of using MpiAdamOptimizer class. 
PPO2 implementation (while with a recent updates it can use MPI as well) uses different version of parallelism. Head process with neural net creates a bunch of subprocesses that run separate enviroments (run environments == take actions and produce next observations and rewards). As @andytwigg describes, the observations and rewards from these multiple environments in subprocesses are batched together. For visual observations, that creates a big enough batch so that computation of NN gradients on a GPU starts making sense (esp. if using convnets). Note that, by default, multiple environments in subprocesses are only used for atari and retro video games, but not, for instance, for mujoco (because observations there are not visual). 
All that being said - ppo1 is now obsolete, and its functionality (including mpi) is fully covered by ppo2. 
Closing this; please reopen if some unclarity remains.

Ok thats your issue then. You need to mark your `/graphql` endpoint as exempt of CSRF protection. You can do this by wrapping the view in the `csrf_exempt` function like this:

```python
# urls.py
from django.views.decorators.csrf import csrf_exempt

urlpatterns = [
    url(r'^graphql$', csrf_exempt(GraphQLView.as_view(schema=schema))),
]
```

There is more information in the Django documentation: https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/ref/csrf/#module-django.views.decorators.csrf

Scott Bogue at Occidental, who has the same system, uses 1.0 because he does not care about the magnetization. Jonathan Glen in an email does care about the magnetization and puts in the volume. He explained to situation to me thus:

I believe Scott routinely puts in 1 because if the samples get mixed up its very difficult to fix the files.  Since Magnetizations is very important for my potential field work, I always put the right value in and triple-check the order of my samples to make sure they don't get mixed up.  I am not sure what a typical Cal tech user does.  One other thing you should be aware of is that the .rmg file has a mistake in it.  The column labeled Moment is really Magnetization (you can confirm this by calculating it from the component values of moment).

I think if the user puts 1.0 because the mass/volume is unspecified, we should honor that and only write out the moment, not put in the wrong magnetization with volume or mass 1.0. A cleaner way to do this would be to put an option flag of, say -mo, for moment only and don't calculate and set the magnetization in that case. This could be a check box in the pmag-gui import interface. What do you think. If you could set it up this way, that would be great.

I took a quick look at this and there is not an `rfftn` or `irfftn` in scipy's fftpack, so I don't think we are missing anything there.  

You are correct that fftconvolve uses rfftn/irfftn when the input data is real-valued, but it [uses the ones from numpy](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/blob/beb258ef9f9b76567f094984f5bfeed774e7e9eb/scipy/signal/signaltools.py#L391-L393) which we do have in our numpy interfaces.

For complex-valued input as in the [example from the docs](http://pyfftw.readthedocs.io/en/latest/source/tutorial.html#monkey-patching-3rd-party-libraries), it should be scipy.fftpack.fftn/scipy.fftpack.ifftn that are used, so I think the example is okay as is.  

We could expand the example to show the correct usage for real-valued data.  I guess one would have to monkeypatch `numpy.fft` to get the speedup in `scipy.fftpack` on real-valued data which is not obvious without inspecting the internals of fftconvolve!  

Also, along these lines, the default numpy, scipy and builder interfaces all use a single thread which is likely not optimal on many modern systems.  I had considered implementing the ability to set an environment variable such as `PYFFTW_NUM_THREADS` that would override this default.  I implemented this recently, but never got around to making a PR for it.  Does that sound like something that would be of interest?  I think the only way to do this now this now would be for the user to roll their own `functools.partial` wrappers around the existing interfaces and monkey patch using those, which is a bit of a pain.

That is, I'm wondering whether we could *just* go with your combined webhook for Mandrill, and not support separate urls for inbound and tracking. That would certainly be simpler, and would only need the existing MANDRILL_WEBHOOK_KEY and MANDRILL_WEBHOOK_URL settings. (Solution 1.)

Under `certbot --help renew`, there is a `--post-hook` flag that runs after all certificates that are due for renewal have been renewed. This flag also works with the `certonly` subcommand and will run after your cert is renewed (it isn't listed under the help for `certonly` due to #1460). If your cert is not due for renewal and you included `--keep-until-expiring` on the command line, `--post-hook` is not run.

This pretty much looks like an RF communication problem. Unfortunately this depends on many factors including antenna tuning (chip to antenna) and coupling (between the two antennas). Most NFC devices are tuned for reader performance, i.e for reading tags. This is called "passive communication mode". Your devices have connected in active communication mode which can be less reliable between identical reader devices. You should try to force passive mode (use --help to find the flag) and could also experiment with explicitly assigning the role of Initiator and Target device.

> If the metadata are supposed to be identifiers only a fixedsize string field would be enough, no?

We already have identifiers for transfers, but the identifier alone doesn't make a nice API to build apps on top. This issue was created to solve the usability of the API.

Think about someone writing an application that uses raiden as a payment method, for this to work with the current API (as of acdf3541e593cb95df996dac4bc034c4abdab341) the payer and the receiving end must use an out-of-band communication channel (e.g. an additional HTTP request), this additional communication step is used to agree on what the payment is for (what service is being payed? Or, what is the content of the online cart?).

The goal here is to make the application developer life easier, allowing him to add arbitrary metadata in the payment with the details his app needs. I think the best approach to solve this problem is to have the metadata delivery only once, when the payment is completed, without sharing this information with the rest of the network.

I think the spec is pretty clear. If no unit is given, it is assumed to be the unit of the current coordinate system, which should usually be pixels. 

The x2 value is of type `<coordinate>`:

> A <coordinate> is a length in the user coordinate system that is the given distance from the origin of the user coordinate system along the relevant axis (the x-axis for X coordinates, the y-axis for Y coordinates). Its syntax is the same as that for <length>.

> For length values in SVG-specific properties and their corresponding presentation attributes, the length unit identifier is optional. If not provided, the length value represents a distance in the current user coordinate system. 

So a value of 1 means 1px not 100% and cannot be removed as done here:

https://github.com/scour-project/scour/blob/57f93efc894b1ba4c30e42b549b0081185029c36/scour/scour.py#L1573

The checks for cx, cy and r seem to share the same problem.

Ahhh, found the source of confusion: They changed the spec!

See [comment #2](https://bugs.launchpad.net/inkscape/+bug/412815) in an old Inkscape bug for Jeffs reason to force Scour to output spaces here. There the grammar reads
```
elliptical-arc-argument:
    nonnegative-number comma-wsp? nonnegative-number comma-wsp?
        number comma-wsp flag comma-wsp flag comma-wsp coordinate-pair
```
i.e. *all* of the three `comma-wsp` in the last line are mandatory.

Since this grammar was never part of the final SVG 1.1 specification and most renderers did never suffer from this issue I tend to drop the behavior altogether. Inkscape still renders this wrongly in 0.91 (the current stable version), though, but will finally have fixed it in the soon to be released Inkscape 0.92.

Anybody feels differently let me know, then we'll keep this workaround as a ``--renderer-workaround`?

ArrayParameter does not have a set attribute, which means we cannot simply use the ArrayParameter class. There are three solutions: 

1) Use the normal Parameter class but store the value as a single string. 
2) Don't use parameters at all for saving/accessing Cartesian/spherical/cylindrical set point but use class methods. The down side of that is that we need separate get/set functions for  cartesian/spherical/cylindrical. 
3) Add a set method on the ArrayParameter class. This is by far the best way, but seeing that this has not been done yet, makes me suspect that this isn't trivial.

Workaround: I installed **valgrind-3.13.0** using linuxbrew. Following http://linuxbrew.sh/, I did

```
sh -c "$(curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Linuxbrew/install/master/install.sh)"

test -d ~/.linuxbrew && PATH="$HOME/.linuxbrew/bin:$HOME/.linuxbrew/sbin:$PATH"
test -d /home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew && PATH="/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/bin:/home/linuxbrew/.linuxbrew/sbin:$PATH"
test -r ~/.bash_profile && echo "export PATH='$(brew --prefix)/bin:$(brew --prefix)/sbin'":'"$PATH"' >>~/.bash_profile
echo "export PATH='$(brew --prefix)/bin:$(brew --prefix)/sbin'":'"$PATH"' >>~/.profile

brew update
brew install valgrind
```

The instructions at http://valgrind.org/downloads/repository.html probably would work as well and may be simpler.

> Is using `wait_task_rescheduled` ok? (Yes it is)

Yeah, you do have to be careful because it exposes some of the raw complexities of async concurrency and doesn't have a lot of guardrails, but this is a case where we're trying to bootstrap some friendly easy-to-use helpers out of nothing, and that's exactly what `wait_task_rescheduled` is intended for.

I can see an argument for including `AsyncioTrioPortal` inside trio -- the main reason I haven't is that I think the trio-asyncio approach is probably better for most users (I think?), and I don't want to confuse people. Even if it doesn't end up in trio itself, then throwing it into another library on PyPI would make sense. It's definitely the sort of thing where one person should implement it once, get it right, and then no-one ever has to look at it again :-).

> I was struggling to figure out how to run an async function inside trio from the outside, since `run_sync_soon` only supports sync functions.

Yeah, this is again because these very-lowest-level tools are as minimal and orthogonal as possible. This is good in some ways, but does make it harder to figure out what you need :-). One trick is to [look at how `BlockingTrioPortal` does it](https://github.com/python-trio/trio/blob/aee2676fd5096daf2849db9756242a30e0aa1f30/trio/_threads.py#L63), but admittedly `BlockingTrioPortal` is also pretty confusing if you don't already know how it works...

>I note that for calling from asyncio to trio, you are using loop.call_soon_threadsafe to bring the result back to the asyncio thread, whereas I was going to use a thread-safe concurrent.futures.Future (with asyncio.wrap_future), which also works. I suppose same difference, but if there is more behind your choice I'd be interested to know.

Yeah, either way would probably work. If you look at [how `wrap_future` is implemented](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/c2b0b12d1a137ada1023ab7c10b8d9a0249d95f9/Lib/asyncio/futures.py#L326-L375), you'll notice that at the end of the day it ends up [calling `loop.call_soon_threadsafe` to resolve an `asyncio.Future`](https://github.com/python/cpython/blob/c2b0b12d1a137ada1023ab7c10b8d9a0249d95f9/Lib/asyncio/futures.py#L359). Since I know this, it felt simpler to just do it myself :-). Also, some of the other ways I use `call_soon_threadsafe` would be more difficult to replace with `wrap_future`. But yeah, for `run_trio_from_asyncio` both ways of doing it are basically equivalent.

-----

@smurfix ah, true, that code I wrote doesn't think at all about how you want to manage the lifetime of the trio thread.

Basically, it assumes you've somehow started a thread running `trio.run`, that you've gotten that `run`'s `TrioToken`, that you've set up some mechanism for keeping the call to `run` sitting idle indefinitely, and that you have some way to shut it down when you're done. For example, you might do something like:

```python
async def trio_main(asyncio_loop, asyncio_queue: asyncio.Queue, asyncio_shutdown_event: asyncio.Event):
    trio_token = trio.hazmat.current_trio_token()
    portal = AsyncioTrioPortal(asyncio_loop, trio_token)
    portal.run_sync_asyncio_from_trio(asyncio_queue.put, portal)
    await portal.run_asyncio_from_trio(asyncio_shutdown_event.wait)
```

So the idea is you start a thread running `trio.run(trio_main, ...)`, passing in the asyncio loop, and a queue and an event (using the asyncio versions of these because during the bootstrapping phase, asyncio is all we have!). Then in the asyncio thread you do `portal = await queue.get()` to get the portal, and then do whatever stuff you're going to do, and eventually, when you're ready to shut things down, you call `shutdown_event.set()`, and this wakes up `trio_main` and causes it to exit.

As soon as it exits, trio will automatically cancel all "system tasks", which includes the tasks we're using to run stuff via `portal.run{,_sync}_trio_from_asyncio`. So you do actually have a way to get them to shut down, once you jump through all the hoops to make it work at all :-). And the portal version has the potential advantage that the trio thread can be doing arbitrary work, without needing a task sitting around waiting for requests to arrive. Maybe that's not so useful in practice though?

OK, let me give one justification for the current design (where initializer is a subset of inputs): if you decide you didn't actually want the pretrained parameters that shipped with the network, you can just drop the initializers on the floor, and you still have a valid network. With the proposed PR, you now have to add extra inputs for everything that was an initializer previously. Also, if initializers actually "define" the tensors, why not just implement them as constant nodes? (One answer: so that the backend can load the constants first, before the inputs are available.)

OTOH, I do agree that the current representation has too many degrees of freedom. For example, the Caffe2 backend currently only accepts inputs positionally. If initializers don't cover a prefix of the inputs, what are the intended semantics? Totally unclear.

In any case, if we accept Rama's PR, we also need to adjust the behavior of the frontends and backends, because Rama's semantics are definitely *not* the current semantics, and they are BC-breaking.

I don't know under what circumstance someone will try to save a string in a ListField but djangotoolbox currently permits it since a string is iterable. To me it appeared that  this feature was there on purpose. Indeed not only it allows to save strings as a ListField without an error, but it also converts the string back to a list when retrieving the document from the database.
 
For showing the current behavior let's say I have this model :

class Test(models.Model):
	evals = ListField()

( col is my collection with pymongo )

>>> b = Test(evals=u'test')
>>> b.save()
>>> col.find_one()
{u'evals': u'test', u'_id': ObjectId('4eec96a594e2e8062c000003')}

As you see there is no error when saving the object even if it breaks integrity since the model requires it to be a list and in the database it is saved as a string. Things are back in order when you get the document back from the database and save it again because it then converts the string in the database to a list when retrieving :

>>> c = Test.objects.get()
>>> c.evals
[u't', u'e', u's', u't']

So one way or the other there is a problem :

1) If you want this feature gone yes I guess throwing an error when submitting anything other than tuple,set or list is the best appropriate way.
2) If this feature stays, well this pull request fixes the flaw !
